Use named variables for control characters so their purpose stays clear in surrounding code.
dont: Assume Chr silently clamps or wraps invalid values. Invalid codes raise an error.
Pass fractional values when exact character selection matters. Chr rounds before converting.
Use unexplained numeric literals repeatedly when a named variable would make the character's purpose clearer.

elements.title: ClearLastSSLError
elements.summary: Clears the current SSL error state.
caveats: This function clears the stored SSL error state only. It does not fix the underlying problem that raised the error.
After the call, GetLastSSLError no longer returns the previously stored error details.

do: Read or log GetLastSSLError before clearing it when the details matter.
Clear the error state at explicit workflow boundaries such as before a retry.
Keep the clear close to the code that depends on a fresh error state.
dont: Clear the error state before you inspect an error you still need.
Treat the .T. return value as proof that an earlier operation succeeded.
Sprinkle this call through unrelated code paths where it can hide the source of a failure.

elements.title: ClearSession
elements.summary: Clears all values from the current session.

exceptions.trigger: The Session public variable is not available in the current execution context.

caveats: After ClearSession runs, GetFromSession returns an empty string for any key that was in the cleared session.
The function expects the Session public variable to exist. If your code can run outside normal session-backed request handling, make sure that context is available before calling ClearSession.

do: Use this function only when you intend to remove all session values.
Call it at clear lifecycle boundaries such as logout or workflow reset.
Pair it with AddToSession and GetFromSession when rebuilding session state after a reset.
dont: Call this function in the middle of a workflow that still depends on existing session values.
Use it when you only need to remove one stored value. ClearSession removes the entire session, not a single key.
Assume it behaves like AddToSession or GetFromSession when Session is unavailable. This function fails instead of falling back silently.

elements.title: ClientEndOfDay
elements.summary: Returns the end of the client's calendar day for a date value.

exceptions.trigger: dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: Argument: dDate must be of date type.

caveats: If the input date does not carry timezone information, the function follows ServerEndOfDay behavior.
If no user timezone offset is available, the server offset is used for the client-side calculation.

do: Use this function for inclusive upper bounds that must follow the client's calendar day.
Handle empty dates deliberately if your workflow distinguishes between no date and a real cutoff.
Use ServerEndOfDay instead when server-defined day boundaries are the real requirement.
dont: Pass strings, numbers, or other non-date values. The function raises an error instead of converting them.
Assume client and server day boundaries are interchangeable. They can differ when timezone offsets differ.
Treat an empty date as a computed end-of-day value. This function returns empty dates unchanged.

elements.title: ClientStartOfDay
elements.summary: Returns the timestamp for the start of the client's calendar day.

exceptions.trigger: dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: Argument: dDate must be of date type.

caveats: If the input date does not carry timezone information, the function follows ServerStartOfDay behavior.
If no user timezone offset is available, the server timezone offset is used for the client-side calculation.

do: Use this function when the user's local day boundary matters more than the server's day boundary.
Pair it with ClientEndOfDay when building an inclusive client-local date range.
Handle empty dates deliberately if your workflow distinguishes between no date and a real cutoff.
dont: Pass strings, numbers, or other non-date values. The function raises an error instead of converting them.
Assume client and server start-of-day values are interchangeable. Different timezone offsets can place the same timestamp on different calendar days.
Treat an empty date as a computed day-start value. This function returns empty dates unchanged.

elements.title: CombineFiles
elements.summary: Concatenates multiple files into one output file on disk.

exceptions.message: Raises a null-argument error for aFileNames.
Raises a null-argument error for sOutFile.

exceptions.trigger: The whitelist configuration denies access to a source or output path.
exceptions.message: Access to folder/file {path} is denied. If system needs access to this folder/file please ask the System Administrator to add the item to WhitelistFolders setting in the configuration file.

exceptions.trigger: sOutFile already exists, because the output file is opened in create-new mode.
A source file cannot be opened for reading.

caveats: If aFileNames is empty, the function still creates an empty output file.
The function concatenates raw file contents in order. It does not insert line breaks, separators, or metadata between files.
If a later source file fails after earlier files were already copied, the output file is not automatically removed or rolled back.

do: Verify that all input files exist and are readable before calling this function.
Choose an output path that does not already exist, or remove the old file first.
Pass the source files in the exact order you want them copied into the result.
Use this function when you need one combined file on disk rather than a combined string in memory.
Treat the operation as byte-for-byte concatenation with no separators added between files.
dont: Assume inaccessible source files will be skipped. The call stops when it cannot open one of the files.

Assume the function rolls back a partially written output file after a later source-file failure.

elements.title: CompArray
elements.summary: Determines whether two arrays are exactly equal.
caveats: Two NIL elements at the same position compare as equal, but NIL on only one side returns .F..
String elements are compared exactly, so matching prefixes are not enough.

do: Use CompArray when you need exact array equality, not just a value lookup.
Validate that both arguments are available before calling when NIL is possible.
Treat a .F. result as a structural or value mismatch and inspect the differing position.
Use CompArray for nested arrays when the full shape must match, not just the top-level length.
dont: Pass NIL for either argument. The function raises an exception instead of returning .F..
Use CompArray when you only need to know whether one value exists somewhere in an array.
Assume two arrays are equal because they contain similar text values. Matching element types are also required.
Assume order does not matter. CompArray compares each position directly.

elements.title: Compress
elements.summary: Compresses a non-empty string and returns the compressed result as either a base64 string or a generated file path.

exceptions.trigger: sSource is empty or not a string value.
exceptions.message: Argument 'sSource' must be a non-empty string.

caveats: File output is created under the application work Temp folder with a generated name.
The generated file name uses a .zip extension even though the content is produced by GZip compression.

Use the default in-memory result when you want to store or transmit compressed text without creating a file.
Pass the returned value to Decompress to restore the original text. For file output, use Decompress(sPath, .T.).
dont: Pass an empty string or a non-string value as sSource.
Assume the return value is a file path unless you called Compress(sSource, .T.).

elements.title: ConvertReport
elements.summary: Converts a report file identified by a file path and returns .T. when the conversion completes.
caveats: ConvertReport never returns .F.. Code that branches on a false return value is misleading because failures are reported as raised errors.

do: Treat a successful return as confirmation that the conversion completed.
Wrap calls in :TRY/:CATCH when a failed conversion should not stop the surrounding workflow.
Log or surface the caught error description when conversion is part of a batch process.
dont: Write logic that expects ConvertReport to return .F. on failure.
Pass a NIL path. That raises an immediate error.
Ignore exceptions in batch or migration code where one bad file should be reported and the rest should continue.

elements.title: CopyToFtp

elements.title: CreateGUID
elements.summary: Generates a new GUID string in uppercase.
caveats: The returned value is uppercase and includes hyphens.
The function returns a string, not a numeric or sequential identifier.

Treat external text as valid GUID data without checking it first.

elements.title: CreateLocal
elements.summary: Creates or overwrites a local variable in the current scope by name.

exceptions.message: Argument sVarName must be a string.

caveats: Local-variable names are resolved case-insensitively.

do: Use CreateLocal when the variable name must be built at runtime.
Use descriptive names so the runtime-created local is easy to identify when reading it later with GetByName.

elements.title: CreateORMSession
elements.summary: Creates the shared ORM session object for the current SSL runtime, or returns the existing one if it has already been created.

exceptions.message: The method OrmSession:UpdatingTable must return a boolean value
Not permitted to update table [tableName] when ORM changes are pending

elements.title: CreatePublic

exceptions.message: Argument sVarName must be a string.

elements.title: CreateUdObject

exceptions.trigger: The first argument is neither a string nor an array.
The second argument in the class-name form is not an array.
A property-definition element is neither a string nor a two-element array.
A property name is empty or not a string.
exceptions.message: Property name must be a non-empty string. Property#: <index>

A property named XmlType is assigned a non-string value.

Use the class-name form only for user-defined :CLASS files.

Omit optional arguments you do not need instead of passing placeholder values.

elements.title: DOW

exceptions.trigger: dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: dDate must be of date type

elements.title: DOY

exceptions.trigger: dDate is NIL or an empty date.
dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: dDate must be of date type

do: Validate uncertain inputs before calling DOY(), especially when empty dates are possible.
Use DOY() when you need year-relative positioning such as seasonal cutoffs, annual schedules, or day-of-year grouping.
Account for leap years when comparing the same calendar period across different years.

Use DOY when you actually need the month, weekday, or full date value.

elements.title: DToC

exceptions.trigger: dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: dDate must be of date type

Treat "  /  /    " as an empty-date marker before displaying or storing the result.
Use DToS or DateToString instead when you need a fixed format.
dont: Pass NIL, strings, or numbers directly. DToC requires a date value.
Assume the result always uses a fixed pattern such as MM/dd/yyyy.
Use DToC for integrations that require a stable interchange format.

elements.title: DToS

exceptions.message: dDate must be of date type

Because the format is year-month-day, simple string sorting also sorts by date.

do: Use DToS when you need a stable yyyyMMdd value for sorting or interchange.
Check for empty dates before or after conversion when blank output would be ambiguous.
Use DToC or DateToString for user-facing formats.
dont: Pass NIL, strings, or numbers directly. DToS requires a date value.
Assume an empty date returns 00000000 or an empty string.
Use DToS for locale-aware or user-formatted output.

elements.title: DateAdd
elements.summary: Adds a time interval to a date and returns the resulting date.

exceptions.trigger: dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: Argument: date must be of date type
Argument number must be a number.

Negative numbers move the date backward instead of forward.

Use whole-number values for year and month additions so the result is predictable.
dont: Pass NIL for dDate or a non-numeric value for nNumber; both raise runtime errors.

elements.title: DateDiff

exceptions.message: Argument: endDate must be of date type

Argument datepart (value=<value>) is invalid. Possible values: day, hour, minute, second, millisecond. <value>` is the invalid string supplied. The message contains a line break between the two sentences.

caveats: "year" and "month" are calendar-based calculations. They do not measure elapsed partial years or partial months.
"day", "hour", "minute", "second", and "millisecond" use elapsed time and truncate partial units toward zero.
sDatepart is normalized to lowercase before validation, so values such as "DAY" and "day" behave the same.
The invalid-sDatepart error text lists only day, hour, minute, second, and millisecond, even though "year" and "month" are also accepted.

do: Pass validated date values for dStartDate and dEndDate.
Specify sDatepart explicitly when the result is not day-based.
Use "month" or "year" when you need calendar-component differences instead of elapsed time.
dont: Use DateDiff when you need fractional durations. It returns whole numbers only.
Estimate months by dividing day counts when calendar month differences matter.
Assume the default unit is anything other than "day".

elements.title: DateDiffEx
elements.summary: Returns the elapsed interval between two date values as an object.

exceptions.trigger: dStartDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: Argument: startDate must be of date type

exceptions.trigger: dEndDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: Argument: endDate must be of date type

Read the interval members you need from the returned object.
Use DateDiffEx when your logic needs a full interval instead of one numeric unit.
dont: Treat the return value as a plain number.
Pass NIL or non-date values for either argument.
Use DateDiffEx when DateDiff already provides the single-unit number you need.

elements.title: DateFormat
elements.summary: Sets the current SSL date format string.

Restore the previous format after export, display, or parsing work that needs a different pattern.
Verify the effective format with LimsGetDateFormat when the exact stored pattern matters.
dont: Assume DateFormat returns the previous format. It always returns an empty string.
Rely on it to validate that a format string is usable for later parsing or formatting.

elements.title: DateFromNumbers
elements.summary: Creates a date value from individual numeric components.

exceptions.message: Argument 'makeInvariant' can be NIL or boolean.

Invalid date combinations such as an out-of-range month or a non-existent calendar day raise a date construction error.

Stop at the last component you actually need instead of passing unnecessary trailing arguments.
Use bMakeInvariant only when your workflow needs an invariant date.
Use DateFromString or StringToDate when the source value starts as text.
dont: Pass fractional numbers such as 2024.5 or 10.25 for date parts.
Assume invalid combinations such as February 30 will be corrected automatically.
Pass strings for numeric parts just because they look numeric.

elements.title: DateFromString
elements.summary: Parses a string into a date value with optional format and culture controls.

exceptions.message: A null argument exception. The exact runtime message is platform-dependent.

Use CToD when the input follows the current SSL date format.

Pass an array of formats when imported data can legitimately arrive in more than one exact shape.
Use useLocalCulture only when the input is intentionally tied to the current local culture.
Handle parse failures with :TRY and :CATCH when invalid input is possible.

Use DateFromString when you want a quiet parse failure. This function raises an error when parsing fails.
Treat bMakeInvariant as time-zone conversion. It changes how the parsed date is marked, not the clock value.

elements.title: DateToString

exceptions.message: Argument: date must be of date type
Argument type must be of type SSLString.

elements.title: Day

Treat a return value of 0 as an empty date case.

exceptions.message: Argument 'sSource' must be a non-empty string.

elements.title: DeleteDirOnFtp

exceptions.message: FTP server name or IP cannot be missing.

exceptions.message: SFTP server name or IP cannot be missing.
Remote folder name cannot be missing.
The proxy parameter cannot be used. The system will automatically use the proxy specified by Internet Explorer > Local Area Network settings.

Treat row[2] as a current document value returned from this function; this API reports type-definition metadata.

elements.title: DocGetTypeAttributesAsDataset
elements.summary: Returns the attributes for a Documentum type as a dataset-formatted string.
caveats: The function does not itself tell you why an empty string was returned. Check DocCommandFailed to distinguish a backend failure from a type that has no attributes.

do: Call DocInitDocumentumInterface before Documentum operations and check the result immediately after this call.
Treat an empty string as an ambiguous result until you also check DocCommandFailed.
Capture DocGetErrorMessage right away when DocCommandFailed() is .T..
dont: Assume "" means only that the type has no attributes.
Pass NIL for sTypeName.
Wait until later in the workflow to inspect the failure state because another Documentum call can replace it.

elements.title: DocGetWorkflowStatus
elements.summary: Returns the current runtime status for a Documentum workflow.

do: Branch on the documented status values and keep a fallback path for "Unknown".
Treat "Finished" as the result for completed workflows and for workflow IDs that are no longer found.
Check DocCommandFailed and DocGetErrorMessage when the function returns an empty string.
dont: Assume an empty string means the workflow finished. It means the lookup failed.
Treat missing workflow IDs as an error case by default. This function maps that case to "Finished".
Pass a NIL workflow identifier.

elements.title: DocGetWorkitemProperties
elements.summary: Retrieves workflow flags and linked document IDs for a Documentum work item.
caveats: The document values are IDs from the work item packages, not expanded document metadata.

do: Read the returned array by fixed position, not by guessing from value type.
Check ALen(aProps) before iterating document IDs from aProps[4] onward.
Wrap the call in :TRY / :CATCH when the work item may be missing or inaccessible.
Treat the first two booleans as current-user workflow permissions, especially for supervisor scenarios.
dont: Assume the document list starts at aProps[1]; the first three entries are always booleans.
Assume aProps[1] and aProps[2] always reflect the stored work item flags without regard to the current user.
Assume every work item has linked documents; the array may contain only three elements.
Build logic that depends on custom labels or metadata in the result; this function returns only positional values.

elements.title: DocImportDocument
elements.summary: Imports a document into Documentum and returns the underlying import result as a string.
caveats: The exact content or format of the success string is not defined here.
The Documentum helper clears the current failure state before the import runs. If the import throws, it stores that exception and the function returns "".

do: Initialize the Documentum interface before importing, and end it when the workflow is complete.
Check DocCommandFailed immediately after the import when failure handling matters.
Capture DocGetErrorMessage before another Documentum call can overwrite the current failure state.
Supply sDocName, sDocType, sAppCode, and sAclName only when your workflow needs those values.
dont: Call this function before DocInitDocumentumInterface has created the current Documentum context.
Rely on the returned string alone to detect failure when your workflow needs certainty.
Pass NIL for sDocFile or sDestinationPath; those arguments raise immediate SSL exceptions.
Wait until later in the workflow to inspect DocCommandFailed or DocGetErrorMessage.

elements.title: DocInitDocumentumInterface
elements.summary: Creates a fresh Documentum interface context for the current execution.
caveats: The new context starts without login information and without a stored Documentum error message.
Calling it again replaces the current Documentum context with a new one.
To inspect login or command failures after later Documentum calls, use DocCommandFailed and DocGetErrorMessage.

do: Call this function once before the first Documentum login or operation in a workflow.
Pair it with DocEndDocumentumInterface when the Documentum work is finished.
Reinitialize explicitly when you want to throw away the current Documentum context and start over.
dont: Treat the NIL return value as a success flag.
Skip initialization and then call login or other Documentum functions against an uninitialized context.
Reinitialize in the middle of a workflow unless you intend to discard the current Documentum context.

elements.title: DocLoginToDocumentum
elements.summary: Authenticates to a Documentum repository for the current initialized Documentum context.
caveats: The current Documentum error state is cleared before the login attempt runs.
A later Documentum call can replace the current failure state and message.

do: Call DocInitDocumentumInterface before DocLoginToDocumentum.
Check the boolean return value immediately and read DocGetErrorMessage right away when the login fails.
Close the Documentum context with DocEndDocumentumInterface when the work is finished.
dont: Call this function before initializing the Documentum interface context.
Continue with other Documentum operations after a .F. result.
Wait until later in the workflow to inspect the failure state, because another Documentum call can replace it.

elements.title: DocPauseWorkflow
elements.summary: Temporarily halts workflow processing to prevent further steps until manual intervention or review.
caveats: The function returns only a boolean result; it does not provide a reason when the pause does not succeed.

do: Validate or obtain the workflow ID before calling the function.
Check the boolean return value and handle .F. explicitly.
Use surrounding workflow logic to decide whether to retry, report, or defer manual review.
dont: Pass NIL for sWorkflowId. That raises an exception.
Assume every workflow ID can be paused successfully.
Ignore a .F. result and continue as if the workflow was paused.

elements.title: DocRemoveAllUsersFromGroup
elements.summary: Removes every user from a Documentum group.
caveats: If the group cannot be updated, the function returns .F..
The boolean result does not identify the specific backend failure.

do: Validate that sGroupName is populated before calling the function.
Check the boolean return value and treat .F. as an operation failure.
Re-add users explicitly after a successful reset if you are rebuilding group membership.
dont: Pass NIL for sGroupName.
Assume a .F. result means only one specific problem such as a missing group.
Use this function when you need to remove only selected users from the group.

elements.title: DocRemoveUsersFromGroup
elements.summary: Removes one or more users from an existing Documentum group.
caveats: If the target group cannot be updated, the function returns .F..
The boolean result is operation-level only. It does not report which user removal failed.

do: Validate that sGroupName and aUsers are populated before calling the function.
Check the boolean return value and treat .F. as an operation failure.
Build the aUsers array from validated user names before calling the function.
dont: Pass NIL for either argument.
Assume the boolean result identifies which user caused the failure.
Assume .F. means only one specific problem such as a missing group.

elements.title: DocRepeatWorkitem
elements.summary: Repeats a Documentum workitem and can reassign it to a new user list.
caveats: A .F. result indicates the repeat did not succeed, but the function does not return a separate failure message.

do: Validate that sWorkitemId and aUsers are assigned before calling the function.
Check the boolean return value and handle .F. explicitly in workflow logic.
Pass the optional signoff values when the repeat must capture approval context.
dont: Pass NIL for sWorkitemId or aUsers. The function raises an error before the repeat is attempted.
Assume the repeat succeeded without checking the return value.
Pass signoff values unless the surrounding process actually requires them.

elements.title: DocResumeWorkflow
elements.summary: Resumes a Documentum workflow identified by sWorkflowId.
caveats: A .F. result indicates that the resume request did not succeed, but the function does not return a separate failure message.

do: Validate that sWorkflowId is assigned before calling the function.
Check the boolean return value and handle .F. explicitly in your workflow logic.
Use DocGetWorkflowStatus or surrounding workflow context when you only want to resume workflows in a known state.
dont: Pass NIL for sWorkflowId. The function raises an error before attempting the resume.
Assume the resume succeeded without checking the return value.
Use ErrorMes for ordinary .F. results when a non-critical user message or branch in logic is enough.

elements.title: DocSearchAsDataset
elements.summary: Searches Documentum and returns the matches as dataset XML.

exceptions.trigger: The underlying query object cannot be obtained.
The underlying Documentum search raises repository or DQL execution errors.
exceptions.message: Repository or DQL execution errors can also propagate from the underlying Documentum search.

caveats: Omitting every filter searches the default object type without folder scoping.
sWhere is appended directly to the generated filter logic, so invalid DQL can fail at runtime.
The return value is XML with schema, so parse it with a dataset-aware workflow before treating it as tabular data.

do: Pass only the filters you need so the generated search stays narrow and predictable.
Check for an empty string before trying to parse or forward the returned dataset XML.
Keep sWhere as a valid DQL condition fragment that can follow WHERE or AND.
Set nResultSetSize when the caller only needs a bounded result set.
dont: Treat the return value as an array. The function returns dataset XML, not row data.
Pass an arbitrary free-form query in sWhere. The function already builds the SELECT and FROM portions.
Leave every filter open for routine searches in large repositories when a folder, type, or result cap is known.

elements.title: DocSearchFullText
elements.summary: Performs a Documentum full-text search and returns matching documents as an array.

exceptions.message: Also rejected by the underlying search call.

do: Validate that sTextToSearch is not blank before calling the function.
Pass sStartLocation when the search should stay inside a known folder tree.
Set nResultSetSize for review queues and pick lists that only need a bounded result set.
Read the returned rows by documented column position so your code stays predictable.
dont: Pass NIL, "", or whitespace-only text as the search value.
Assume nResultSetSize paginates results. It only requests a maximum row count.
Search the entire repository when a folder path is already known.
Assume a match exists before checking ALen(aResults).

elements.title: DocSearchUsingDql
elements.summary: Executes a Documentum DQL query and returns the result set as a two-dimensional array.
caveats: An empty string DQL value is rejected by the underlying Documentum search call. In that case SSL receives an empty array and the failure details are available through DocCommandFailed and DocGetErrorMessage.
Call DocInitDocumentumInterface before using Documentum functions.

do: Select only the columns your script actually uses so the returned row shape stays simple and predictable.
Check DocCommandFailed and DocGetErrorMessage when ALen(aResults) == 0 and you need to know whether the search failed.
Pass nResultSetSize for review queues, lookups, and other flows that do not need the full result set.
Read row values by their documented query position, such as aResults[nIndex, 2] for the second selected column.
dont: Treat each result row as an object with named properties. Each row is an array.
Pass NIL for sDql. That raises an exception instead of returning a result.
Assume nResultSetSize paginates results. It only requests a top-N limit.
Assume every empty result means no documents matched. A failed Documentum call also returns an empty array.

elements.title: DocSetMetadata
elements.summary: Updates one or more metadata attributes on a Documentum object.
caveats: The SSL entry point documents argument validation only for NIL values. Blank strings, unknown attribute names, and other backend-specific validation outcomes are handled by the Documentum call, not by a separate SSL pre-check.

do: Build aAttributes as an array of two-item arrays so each row clearly maps an attribute name to a value.
Check DocCommandFailed and DocGetErrorMessage immediately after a .F. return.
Group related metadata changes into one call when they target the same object.
Initialize the Documentum interface before Documentum API work in scripts that manage their own session lifecycle.
dont: Pass NIL for sObjId or aAttributes; both raise an error before any metadata update is attempted.
Assume a .F. return explains the failure by itself; inspect DocGetErrorMessage while the error state is still current.
Pass rows that are not {name, value} pairs; the function expects each array element to provide the attribute name first and the value second.
Split one logical metadata update across multiple calls unless you need separate success handling for each call.

elements.title: DocStartWorkflow
elements.summary: Starts a Documentum workflow and returns the created workflow ID together with the start-activity performers.

exceptions.message: You must specify workflow's path or workflow's id!

exceptions.trigger: The current user cannot start the workflow.
exceptions.message: The current user has no permissions to start the workflow!

exceptions.trigger: The workflow start path requires documents but none are supplied.
exceptions.message: The workflow requires at least one document!

caveats: A workflow can start without a document package only when its start path does not require one.
The returned workflow ID is the created Documentum workflow instance ID, not necessarily the same text you passed in as sWorkflowId.

do: Pass aDocumentIds as an array, even when you are attaching only one document.
Read the returned values with SSL's 1-based indexing: aWorkflowInfo[1] for the workflow ID and aWorkflowInfo[2] for performers.
Omit sPackageName when the default package name is acceptable.
dont: Document or code against zero-based positions such as index 0. SSL arrays are 1-based.
Assume aDocumentIds is always optional. Some workflows require at least one document at startup.
Assume aWorkflowInfo[2] always contains a populated performer array. Check the returned value before iterating it.

elements.title: DocStopWorkflow
elements.summary: Stops a Documentum workflow by its workflow ID.
caveats: This page documents a required non-NIL argument. The wrapper does not add a separate empty-string check.
The function depends on an active Documentum interface context.

do: Initialize the Documentum interface before calling this function.
Check the boolean result immediately after the call.
After a .F. result, use DocCommandFailed and DocGetErrorMessage for diagnostics.
dont: Pass NIL for sWorkflowId.
Assume .F. tells you why the stop request failed.
Make another Documentum call before reading the current error state when a stop fails.

elements.title: DocUpdateUser
elements.summary: Updates a Documentum user and returns the backend result message.

do: Validate that sLoginName and sPassword are assigned before calling the function.
Omit trailing optional arguments you do not need, and skip only the positions required to reach a later argument.
Check whether the returned string is empty before treating the update as confirmed.
dont: Pass NIL for sLoginName or sPassword. Those arguments raise an immediate error instead of attempting the update.
Assume omitted optional arguments have a documented repository-side effect beyond being forwarded as omitted values.
Ignore an empty return string when later logic depends on a confirmed update result.

elements.title: DosSupport
elements.summary: Executes operating system-level file and directory commands.

exceptions.trigger: sPrm is NIL for commands other than WORK, WORKDIR, and CURRENTDRIVE.
sCmd is not a supported command.
DIR, MD, CD, RD, or ISDIR targets a non-whitelisted path.

caveats: Passing NIL for sPrm raises an exception for most commands, even when an empty string would be acceptable.
DIR returns raw row arrays, not objects.

do: Use WORK or WORKDIR to read the current directory instead of trying to infer it from CD.
Check the boolean result from MD, CD, and RD before continuing with later file operations.
Use explicit DIR attribute filters when you need directories, hidden items, or system items in the result.
dont: Treat the third argument (vDbg) as a universal debug message. Only MD, CD, and RD use a boolean debug flag, while DIR uses that position for attribute filtering.
Assume RD returning .T. means a directory was actually removed. It also returns .T. when the target does not exist.
Call path-based commands against locations outside the configured whitelist.

elements.title: Empty
elements.summary: Returns .T. when a value is considered empty by SSL; otherwise returns .F.

do: Use Empty() for general-purpose validation when the value type may vary.
Use Empty() after parsing or lookup operations that return empty dates, blank strings, or empty arrays.
Combine Empty() with explicit object-property checks when validating structured objects.
dont: Assume Empty() treats every object with no visible data as empty. Regular SSL objects still return .F..
Use only a null check when SSL also treats 0, .F., blank strings, and empty dates as empty.
Use Empty() when 0 or .F. are meaningful business values that must stay distinct from missing input.

elements.title: EncryptData
elements.summary: Encrypts a string with a password by using the legacy built-in RC2, DES, or 3DES algorithms.

exceptions.trigger: sAlgorithm is not RC2, DES, or 3DES.
sKey is neither a valid integer string nor MAX.
The numeric key size is outside the range supported by the selected algorithm.
exceptions.message: Invalid key size: {key}. Min size: {min}, Max size: {max}, Skip size: {skip}

caveats: sKey is a key length setting such as 128 or MAX, not the password text itself.
The public signature includes sRetType, but the return format is always the STARLIMS-formatted encrypted string expected by DecryptData.

do: Use the same password for both EncryptData and DecryptData.
Validate that sInputData and sPassword are not empty before treating the result as a usable encrypted value.
Standardize the algorithm and key length across the systems that produce and consume the encrypted value.
dont: Use this function for one-way password hashing. Use HashData when the value should not be decrypted later.
Assume an empty return value means encryption succeeded with an empty payload. It also means sInputData or sPassword was empty.
Pass arbitrary algorithm or key values from user input without validation. Invalid values raise runtime errors.

elements.title: EndLimsOleConnect
elements.summary: Disposes an object previously created for OLE automation use.
caveats: If oConnection is not an OLE automation object, the function skips cleanup and still returns an empty string.

do: Pair LimsOleConnect with EndLimsOleConnect in the same workflow.
Keep cleanup near the code that creates the automation object.
Prefer a :FINALLY block when later statements might fail.
dont: Leave OLE automation objects undisposed after use. That can keep external resources alive longer than intended.
Assume this function validates that the object came from the expected ProgID or automation server. It only performs the cleanup step for OLE automation objects.
Use repeated cleanup calls as a control-flow signal. The function's documented contract is disposal plus an empty-string return value, not a status report.

elements.title: EndLimsTransaction
elements.summary: Ends a LIMS transaction on the default connection or on a named connection.

exceptions.trigger: The named connection does not exist.

exceptions.message: The internal database collection is null

exceptions.trigger: The database provider cannot close the transaction.

caveats: To pass bCommit while using the default connection, skip sConnectionName with EndLimsTransaction(, bCommit).

do: Call EndLimsTransaction exactly once for every successful BeginLimsTransaction call.
Keep a bCommit flag that only becomes .T. after all transactional work succeeds.
Use the same sConnectionName that started the transaction when you are working on a named connection.
Roll back the outer transaction when an inner transaction on the same connection rolls back.
dont: Assume the function returns .F. for a missing connection or failed close; those paths raise errors.
Mix connection names between BeginLimsTransaction and EndLimsTransaction calls.
Commit in :FINALLY before the protected work has finished successfully.
Ignore nested rollback state and still try to commit the outermost transaction.

elements.title: ErrorMes
elements.summary: Logs an error message and returns the resulting message string.
caveats: Additional formatting details are not documented, so rely on the returned string and the fact that the message is written.

do: Pass clear string values for the caption and detail text.
Use ErrorMes for failures that must be written even when normal user-message logging is not enough.
In :CATCH blocks, log the :Description value from GetLastSSLError so the stored message contains the runtime error text.
dont: Use ErrorMes for routine status updates or non-critical notices. Prefer UsrMes or InfoMes for non-error messaging.
Pass arrays or objects unless their string form is exactly what you want recorded.
Rely on undocumented formatting details beyond the fact that it behaves like UsrMes with forced writing enabled.

elements.title: Eval
elements.summary: Invokes a code block with the supplied arguments and returns the block's result.

exceptions.trigger: fnCode is not a code block.

caveats: Errors raised inside the invoked block propagate to the Eval call site; wrap the call in :TRY / :CATCH when the block may fail.
Eval forwards the supplied argument array to the code block; it does not compile source text, rename parameters, or add its own defaults.

do: Pass a code block value (from a {|params| expr} literal or a function that returns one) as the first argument.
Reserve use of Eval for scenarios requiring dynamic, runtime code evaluation.
Handle errors that may arise from evaluated code blocks.
dont: Pass an SSL source string to Eval. Strings are not code blocks, so the runtime rejects them when eval is invoked on that value type. Use ExecUdf when you need to execute source text.
Use Eval in place of ordinary static routines or built-in functions. Standard routines are more performant and easier to debug.
Assume all evaluated code will succeed without handling errors. Error handling prevents runtime failures and unexpected halts.

elements.title: ExecFunction
elements.summary: Invokes a function by name at runtime and returns the result.

exceptions.trigger: Called with no arguments at all.
exceptions.message: Please provide at least one parameter for ExecFunction

exceptions.trigger: aParameters is provided but is not an array.

caveats: If the function name is misspelled, empty, or references a non-existent target, a runtime error is thrown with little context.
All type validation is deferred to the time of invocation, so incorrect argument types or counts are only caught at runtime.

do: Verify that the function name exists and expects the correct arguments before calling.
When passing arguments, provide them as an array.
Handle errors from this function proactively to manage cases where the call fails or the function does not exist.
dont: Allow untrusted input to determine the function name or arguments without validation.
Omit the second argument or provide a non-array value when the target expects arguments.
Assume all calls will succeed or ignore errors from dynamic invocation.

elements.title: ExecInternal
elements.summary: Calls a method on an object by name and returns that method's result.
caveats: ExecInternal does not define its own method-specific validation beyond the NIL checks for o and sMethodName.
Trailing empty argument slots are removed before the target method is called.
The result type and any additional runtime errors depend on the target object and method being invoked.

do: Validate that both o and sMethodName are present before calling.
Omit unused trailing arguments instead of padding the call.
Use GetInternal or SetInternal when you only need dynamic property access.
dont: Pass NIL for o or sMethodName. Both raise an immediate runtime error.
Use ExecInternal when a direct member call is clearer and already known at design time.
Assume ExecInternal will fix an invalid method name or incompatible argument list for you.

elements.title: ExecUdf
elements.summary: Executes SSL source supplied as a string and returns the result.

exceptions.trigger: aArgs is provided but is not an array.
sCode cannot be compiled or does not produce a runnable entry point.

caveats: Omitting aArgs is valid. To set bCacheCode without arguments, skip the second parameter with adjacent commas: ExecUdf(sCode,, .T.).

do: Pass a complete expression or a complete SSL snippet with an explicit :RETURN when the result matters.
Skip aArgs with adjacent commas when you only want to set the cache flag, for example ExecUdf(sCode,, .T.).
Use caching only for stable source text that will be executed repeatedly.
Wrap dynamic execution in :TRY / :CATCH when the source may fail to compile or run.
dont: Pass a string, number, or object as the second argument. aArgs must be an array when present.
Use ExecUdf when Eval or DoProc already fits the job better. Eval is for code blocks, and DoProc is for known procedures.
Assume generated code is safe just because it comes from your own script. Validate or constrain dynamic source before executing it.
Enable bCacheCode for one-off or constantly changing code strings where reuse provides no benefit.

elements.title: ExtractCol
elements.summary: Extracts one column from a two-dimensional array and returns the extracted values as a new array.

exceptions.trigger: nColumn is NIL and aTarget is not empty.

exceptions.message: Column number must be an integer value.
Column number cannot be less than one.

exceptions.trigger: Any row is not an array.
exceptions.message: Target must be an array of arrays (a two dimensional array).

exceptions.trigger: nColumn exceeds the length of any row.
exceptions.message: Column number cannot exceed the number of elements in any sub-array.

caveats: An empty aTarget returns an empty array immediately, before nColumn is validated.

do: Validate nColumn and the shape of aTarget before calling.
Use one-based column indexing as required.
Check for an empty result before chaining the extracted values into later work.
dont: Assume every row has the same shape. A short or malformed row raises an error for the whole call.
Use zero-based or negative indices. Indexes less than one will always raise an error.
Pass a one-dimensional array. ExtractCol expects each top-level element to be a row array.

elements.title: ExtractZip
elements.summary: Extracts a ZIP archive into a target directory.
caveats: Errors from invalid paths, invalid filter expressions, ZIP processing, and decryption propagate to the caller.

do: Validate the archive path and destination folder before calling ExtractZip.
Omit optional arguments you do not need instead of passing placeholder values.
Use a filter expression when you only need a subset of archived files.
Wrap extraction in :TRY / :CATCH when the archive, path, password, or filter may fail at runtime.
dont: Assume existing files in the destination are preserved. ExtractZip extracts with overwrite enabled.
Use shell-style wildcard examples such as *.csv. sFileFilter uses case-insensitive regular expressions against archived file paths.
Assume a file-only filter prevents empty directory creation. Empty directory entries can still be created from the archive.
Use ErrorMes for routine success reporting. Reserve it for failures that must be logged.

elements.title: FileSupport
elements.summary: Performs multiple file operations through a single request-driven interface.

exceptions.trigger: vFileIdentifier is neither a string nor a number.
exceptions.message: Request argument does not contain a string/numeric!
Request argument does not contain a string!

exceptions.trigger: sEncoding is provided but is not a string.
exceptions.message: Argument: <encoding> must be of type string.

exceptions.trigger: sRequest does not match any supported request family.
The byte count for READ is negative.
CHECK, SETATTR, GETATTR, CREATE, OPEN, COPY, DELETE, MOVE, RENAME, or DIR targets a path outside the configured whitelist.
exceptions.message: Access to folder/file {path} is denied. If system needs access to this folder/file please ask the System Administrator to add the item to WhitelistFolders setting in the configuration file.

caveats: DIR returns files only. Unlike Directory, it does not expose attribute filtering and does not include hidden files.
Using the wrong argument type for a specific request can raise a runtime type error before the underlying file operation runs.

do: Use CREATE or OPEN first, keep the returned handle, and close it with CLOSE when you finish.
Use the canonical request names such as "READ", "WRITE", and "SEEK" even though the implementation matches by leading text.
Check the boolean result from COPY, DELETE, MOVE, RENAME, and CLOSE before assuming the operation succeeded.
Wrap path-based operations in :TRY / :CATCH when the target path may fall outside the configured whitelist.
dont: Pass a file path to handle-based requests such as READ or WRITE, or a handle to path-based requests such as COPY or DIR.
Assume READ returns a short string at end of file. It pads the result with spaces when fewer characters are available.
Treat RENAME as a metadata-only rename. It uses the same move logic as MOVE and therefore depends on a destination path.
Rely on non-canonical request prefixes in published code just because the implementation accepts leading-text matches.

elements.title: FormatErrorMessage
elements.summary: Returns a formatted string description for an error value.
caveats: Custom error types not based on the standard SSLError structure produce "Unknown error.", not their specific fields.

do: Always use this function before displaying or logging error values of unknown type.
Use this along with error-handling flows that may produce non-error values, especially in catch-all logic.
Test with both error and non-error values to validate the user experience of error reporting.
dont: Assume every input produces a meaningful description — non-SSLError values always fall back to "Unknown error.".
Cast or convert error objects to string directly; direct conversion may produce technical or unhelpful output.
Expect nested or compound error details; only the top-level description is returned.

elements.title: FormatSqlErrorMessage
elements.summary: Returns a human-readable error message from a SQL error value.
caveats: The returned message is only as descriptive as the error object provided; incomplete or malformed errors yield less helpful text.

do: Always use this function before displaying or logging SQL errors.
Handle the possibility of "Unknown error." as a return value in your user interface.
Use in combination with error retrieval functions like GetLastSQLError.
dont: Extract message properties directly from the error object; use this function to ensure consistent formatting and avoid runtime errors with unexpected value types.
Assume all errors will contain detailed or helpful information — non-SSLError values always fall back to "Unknown error.".
Rely solely on raw SQL error output in user-facing processes.

elements.title: FromJson
elements.summary: Parses a JSON string into the closest SSL-native value or returns the input unchanged if it is not a string. JSON arrays become arrays, objects become SSLExpando instances, numbers become numbers, booleans become booleans, and strings become strings or dates when the value starts with SSLDate|. Null input, empty strings, and JSON null values return NIL. Invalid JSON tokens raise an error.

exceptions.trigger: An unknown or invalid token is encountered while parsing the JSON string.

caveats: Input JSON objects become SSLExpando instances and do not implement all methods of a standard SSL object.
Extra whitespace and line breaks are tolerated by the parser, but stray text before or after valid JSON raises an error.

do: Validate that input data is a valid JSON string before calling this function.
Pair FromJson with ToJson when round-tripping data between SSL and JSON.
dont: Assume all incoming values are parseable without checking.
Use on data serialized with custom delimiters or non-JSON formats.

elements.title: FromXml
elements.summary: Parses a type-tagged XML string and converts it to the corresponding SSL value.

exceptions.trigger: The string cannot be parsed as valid XML.
An element inside <complextype> does not match the declared element type.
exceptions.message: Incorrect element type {element} found in array of type {typeName}

exceptions.trigger: The <complextype> contents cannot be converted to the target array type.
An element's text cannot be converted to its declared type.
The root element is not one of the recognized type tags.

caveats: <dto> elements are restored via SSLExpando deserialization and may not implement all methods of a hand-built object.
A <date> element whose text is 00/00/0000 returns NIL, not a zero date.

do: Use ToXml to produce the XML string and FromXml to restore it — this guarantees the tag format is correct.
Handle exceptions when parsing XML from external sources in case the format is invalid or unexpected.
Check for NIL on return to distinguish empty, null, and zero-date inputs.
dont: Pass hand-authored XML with arbitrary tag names. Only the recognized type tags produce a result; all others raise an error.
Assume the return type without checking — the tag determines the type and multiple valid inputs can return NIL.

elements.title: GetAppBaseFolder
elements.summary: Returns the application's base folder path as a string for use in file and configuration operations.

do: Use GetRegionEx only when you genuinely need local overrides. Prefer GetRegion for normal scope-based lookups.

elements.title: GetSSLDataset
elements.summary: Executes a SQL statement and returns the result as an SSLDataset.

exceptions.trigger: aParamNames has more entries than aParamValues.
exceptions.message: Not enough values provided for parameters.

exceptions.trigger: The connection name is not configured.

exceptions.message: The internal database collection is null.

Mix column names and numeric ordinals in the same aInvariantDateCols array.

elements.title: GetSetting
elements.summary: Retrieves a single named setting.
caveats: Because a missing setting and a configured empty-string setting both surface as "", GetSetting alone cannot distinguish those cases.

do: Pass a real setting name string, not NIL.
Check the returned value before using it in type-sensitive logic.
Use LimsTypeEx when the setting may hold different value types.

elements.title: GetSettings

exceptions.trigger: aNames is NIL or an empty array.

Keep the input names in the order you want to consume the results.
Check individual returned values before using them in type-sensitive logic.
Use LimsTypeEx when a returned setting may not always have the same type.

Use Empty() by itself to decide whether a value is missing when 0 or .F. are valid configured values.

elements.title: GetTables
elements.summary: Extracts table names from the FROM portion of a SQL SELECT string.
caveats: The function is case-insensitive when locating the SELECT, FROM, and stop keywords.

do: Pass a normal SELECT statement when you want predictable results.
Treat the result as a text-parsing aid, then apply any validation you need separately.
Expect an empty array when the SQL does not contain a recognizable SELECT ... FROM section.
dont: Use it as a database-existence check. It parses SQL text only.
Assume it understands every SQL construct beyond the first FROM section it scans.

elements.title: GetTransactionsCount
elements.summary: Returns the number of open database transactions for a specified or default connection.

exceptions.trigger: sConnection is provided but is not a string.
sConnection names a connection that does not exist.

caveats: The function only reports the current transaction count; it does not start, commit, or roll back transactions.
An invalid connection name raises an error instead of returning 0.

do: Use the default connection when you only need the current script context.
Check the count before calling EndLimsTransaction in defensive transaction-handling code.
Pass an explicit connection name when your script works with more than one database connection.
dont: Pass numbers, arrays, or objects as sConnection.
Assume a named connection exists without validating the connection name used by your environment.
Use this function as a replacement for BeginLimsTransaction, EndLimsTransaction, or IsInTransaction.

elements.title: GetUserData
elements.summary: Returns the current session user name as a string.
caveats: GetUserData() returns the current session value only; it does not look up other users.
If the current session user changes, later calls return the updated value.

do: Use GetUserData() when you need the current session user for logging, audit text, or workflow decisions.
Save the current user with GetUserData() before temporarily changing the session context with SetUserData.
Check for an empty result before treating the value as a usable user name.
dont: Use GetUserData() as proof that a user has been authenticated for a sensitive action.
Assume the returned value is non-empty without checking it.
Hard-code logic that depends on a previous user value after another part of the session may have changed it with SetUserData.

elements.title: GetWebFolder
elements.summary: Returns the current web folder path as a string.
caveats: Any separator handling or path normalization must be done by the caller.

do: Use GetWebFolder when you need the configured web-root path rather than a hard-coded location.
Verify the returned value before using it in later file operations.
Normalize path separators in your own code if you need to append relative paths safely.
dont: Hard-code the web folder path in scripts.
Assume the returned folder exists or is writable just because the function returned a string.
Use GetWebFolder for work or log storage when GetAppWorkPathFolder or GetLogsFolder is the better match.

elements.title: HasProperty
elements.summary: Checks whether a value exposes a named property.
caveats: If the supplied value does not support property lookup, the call can raise a runtime error instead of returning .F..

do: Use HasProperty() before reading optional properties from dynamic objects.
Keep property names consistent across AddProperty, HasProperty(), and SetByName calls.
Treat HasProperty() as a presence check only, then read the value separately.
dont: Pass NIL for oTarget or sPropName; the function raises an error instead of returning .F..
Use HasProperty to fetch a value. It only tells you whether the property exists.
Assume every SSL value supports property lookup. Values without properties can raise a runtime error.

elements.title: HashData
elements.summary: Computes a one-way hash string from input text.

exceptions.message: HashData`. (Note: the runtime uses the function name as the exception message text — it is not descriptive.)

caveats: sAlgorithm is case-insensitive, but the accepted values are still limited to SH1 and MD5.
HashData("") is valid and returns the hash of the empty string.
The same input hashed with SH1 and MD5 produces different results.

do: Standardize on one algorithm for a given workflow so stored hashes remain comparable.
Handle runtime errors when sInputData may be NIL or sAlgorithm may come from external input.
Record which algorithm produced the hash when values may be shared across systems.
dont: Pass algorithm names such as SHA1 or SHA256. HashData only accepts SH1 and MD5.

Use HashData when you need to recover the original value later. Use EncryptData for reversible storage.

elements.title: Hour
elements.summary: Extracts the hour component from a date value.

exceptions.trigger: dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: Argument: dDate must be of date type

caveats: Midnight also returns 0, so the result alone does not distinguish midnight from an empty date.

do: Pass a real date value before calling Hour.
Treat a returned 0 carefully when empty dates are possible, because midnight also returns 0.
Combine Hour with Minute or Second when hour-only precision is not enough.
dont: Pass NIL and expect 0; NIL raises an error.
Pass strings or other non-date values and expect implicit conversion.
Assume 0 always means midnight when your input may be empty.

elements.title: HtmlDecode
elements.summary: Converts selected HTML/XML entity sequences in a string back to literal characters.
caveats: An empty string returns an empty string.

do: Use HtmlDecode when you need to reverse text that was previously encoded with HtmlEncode.
Check for NIL when your calling code distinguishes between no value and an empty string.
Treat the decoded result as plain text unless you have separately validated it for HTML rendering.
dont: Assume HtmlDecode sanitizes content. It only converts supported entity sequences back to literal characters.
