elements.title: HtmlEncode
elements.summary: Converts selected characters in a string to entity sequences for HTML or XML text output.
caveats: An empty string returns an empty string.
Already encoded text is encoded again. For example, & becomes &amp;.

do: Encode text at the point where you insert it into HTML or XML output.
Keep the original unencoded value when that same data may also be needed for plain text, storage, or later editing.
Use HtmlDecode only when you specifically need to undo the five replacements performed by HtmlEncode.
dont: Encode the same value multiple times unless you intentionally want sequences like &amp;.
Assume HtmlEncode performs general input validation or business rule checks. It only replaces five characters.
Assume it converts every named HTML entity or every non-ASCII character. Characters outside the five supported replacements are left as-is.

elements.title: IIf
elements.summary: Selects one of two values based on a boolean condition.

exceptions.trigger: The call does not supply exactly three arguments.
exceptions.message: Invalid number of arguments for function IIF.

caveats: IIf is a normal function call. Its arguments are evaluated before the function returns either vTrueValue or vFalseValue.
The compiler validates IIf calls specially and raises a compile-time error when the call does not contain exactly three arguments.
The compiler can warn when the second or third argument is not a constant or a variable name.

do: Use IIf for short, direct two-way value selection.
Pass a boolean expression for bCondition.
Prefer literals or previously assigned variables for vTrueValue and vFalseValue.
dont: Pass NIL as bCondition.
Rely on IIf to avoid evaluating the unused branch.
Build long nested IIf chains when a regular :IF block is clearer.

elements.title: IgnoreSqlErrors
elements.summary: Enables or disables SQL error suppression and returns the previous setting.
caveats: The setting remains in effect for later SQL operations until you change it again.
If you forget to restore the previous value, unrelated SQL work later in the same execution flow may continue with suppression still enabled.
SQL error suppression is not a substitute for validation, transaction control, or explicit post-operation checks.
This setting works together with ShowSqlErrors; some SQL errors classified as fatal may still be raised when SQL error display is enabled.

do: Save the returned value and restore it after the temporary SQL work is complete.
Limit suppression to a small, clearly defined block of database operations.
Add a brief comment explaining why the SQL work is intentionally best-effort.
dont: Leave SQL error suppression enabled longer than necessary because later SQL failures can be hidden.
Use this as a default error-handling strategy because it can mask real database problems.
Change this flag without considering ShowSqlErrors when you need predictable SQL error visibility.

elements.title: InBatchProcess
elements.summary: Returns whether the current SSL execution context is running in a batch process.
caveats: InBatchProcess() only tells you whether the current context is in batch mode.
The function does not identify a batch job or provide batch metadata.

do: Use InBatchProcess() to choose between batch-safe and interactive behavior.
Use quieter logging in batch code when a popup or prompt would be inappropriate.
Keep the branch close to the behavior it controls so the context decision stays easy to follow.
dont: Track batch state with your own flag when the runtime can answer it directly.
Assume code always runs interactively if the same routine can also be submitted to batch.
Use ErrorMes for ordinary context reporting when UsrMes or InfoMes is sufficient.

elements.title: InfoMes
elements.summary: Logs an informational user message and returns the same formatted string as UsrMes.
caveats: Both arguments are converted to strings before the message is processed.
If vCaption is empty, a default user-message caption is used.
If vMessage is empty, the value from vCaption becomes the message body and the caption is reset to the default user-message caption.
InfoMes follows the same write behavior as UsrMes, so it can return an empty string when ordinary user-message logging is disabled.

do: Pass a short caption in vCaption and the detail text in vMessage.
Use InfoMes for routine progress notes, confirmations, and other non-error messages.
Use ErrorMes when the message must still be written during error handling.
dont: Use InfoMes for failures that must always be recorded.
Pass arrays or objects unless their string form is exactly what you want returned and logged.
Rely on InfoMes as a guaranteed audit mechanism when ordinary user-message logging may be disabled.

elements.title: Integer
elements.summary: Returns the whole-number portion of a numeric value by truncating the fractional part toward zero.
caveats: If you are starting from text input, convert it to a number first.

do: Use Integer when you specifically want truncation toward zero.
Validate string input before converting it to a number and passing it to Integer.
Use Round instead when you need rounding rather than truncation.
dont: Treat Integer as a rounding function. It removes the fractional part instead.
Pass NIL and expect a blank or default numeric result.
Assume Integer(-2.7) behaves like floor logic. It returns -2, not -3.

elements.title: IsDBConnected
elements.summary: Checks whether STARLIMS currently has a database connection available for a given connection name.

exceptions.trigger: The runtime database collection has not been initialized.
exceptions.message: The internal database collection is null

caveats: If the connection name does not match a known connection, the function returns .F..
If runtime database state is unavailable, the function raises an error instead of returning .F..
Passing an empty string checks the default connection, which may not be the named connection you intended to inspect.

do: Use IsDBConnected() before database work when your script needs a fast availability check.
Pass an explicit connection name when your script works with more than one connection.
Wrap the call in :TRY / :CATCH when the runtime context may not have database state available.
dont: Treat this as a connectivity probe that validates credentials, network reachability, or a successful round-trip query.
Pass an empty string when you mean to verify a specific non-default connection by name.
Assume the function always returns .F. on failure; it can raise an error when runtime database state is unavailable.

elements.title: IsDefined
elements.summary: Determines whether a variable name is currently defined.

exceptions.trigger: sVarName is not a string or is empty.
exceptions.message: Argument 'sVarName' must be a non-empty string!

caveats: Variable lookup follows normal SSL variable resolution: current local scope,
The name comparison is case-insensitive, consistent with SSL variable names.
IsDefined() checks whether the variable exists, not whether its value is empty.

do: Pass a runtime variable name string, not the variable value itself.
Use IsDefined() before GetByName when a variable may or may not exist.
Use Empty separately when you need to know whether a defined variable also has content.
dont: Pass NIL, "", or a non-string value and expect .F.. Those inputs raise exceptions.
Use IsDefined() to check object properties such as oConfig:ModuleName; it checks variable names, not member paths.
Use Empty as a substitute for existence checks. A variable can be defined and still be empty.

elements.title: IsGuid
elements.summary: Validates whether a string matches the GUID format and returns a boolean result.
caveats: Empty strings return .F..
The format must include hyphens in the standard 8-4-4-4-12 layout; a 32-character hex string without hyphens is not accepted.

do: Validate external identifier strings before using them in lookup or save logic.
Guard values that may be NIL before calling IsGuid.
Accept both uppercase and lowercase GUID text when the format is otherwise valid.
dont: Assume any non-empty identifier string is valid without checking it.
Pass NIL and expect .F. because the function raises an error for missing input.
Reject a GUID only because it uses lowercase hex digits or surrounding braces.

elements.title: IsHex
elements.summary: Validates whether a string contains only uppercase hexadecimal characters.
caveats: Lowercase a-f is rejected. Use Upper first if you want to accept mixed-case input.
Leading prefixes such as 0x, whitespace, and punctuation cause the check to fail.
The empty string returns .T.. Add a separate length check when at least one hex digit is required before conversion.

do: Check that sSource is not NIL before calling IsHex.
Use Upper first when your input may contain lowercase a-f.
Add a length check when the next step requires at least one hex digit.
Validate untrusted hex input before sending it to downstream conversion logic.
dont: Assume NIL input returns .F.. It raises an error instead.
Assume lowercase a-f is accepted. IsHex only accepts uppercase A-F.
Skip validation when later logic expects a hex-only string.

elements.title: IsInTransaction
elements.summary: Returns .T. if the specified database connection currently has an open transaction.

exceptions.trigger: The runtime database collection has not been initialized.
exceptions.message: The internal database collection is null

caveats: If vConnection is NIL, empty, or missing, the default connection is used.
vConnection can be a connection name or a SQLConnection object returned by GetConnectionByName.
Passing an unknown connection name raises an error rather than returning .F..
If the internal database collection is unavailable, an error is raised and the function does not return a value.
Only tests direct transaction state for the specified connection — does not account for nested or distributed transactions.

do: Pass explicit, known-good connection names when checking transaction state.
Confirm a transaction is active before issuing commit or rollback commands.
Use with BeginLimsTransaction and EndLimsTransaction for end-to-end transaction management.
dont: Pass user-supplied or dynamic connection names without validating them first. Invalid names raise errors.
Use this function as a transaction manager or depth counter. It checks boolean state only.
Assume the function returns .F. for unknown connections. It raises an error instead.

elements.title: IsInvariantDate
elements.summary: Checks whether a date value has an unspecified (invariant) kind.

exceptions.trigger: dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: Argument: date must be of date type

caveats: This function does not convert or mutate the input value — it only inspects it.

do: Always validate that your input is a date before calling this function.
Use IsInvariantDate to clearly separate invariant date logic from local and UTC handling.
Combine with MakeDateInvariant or MakeDateLocal for comprehensive date workflows.
dont: Assume that user-provided or external values are already of date type — validate before calling to avoid runtime errors.
Attempt to infer date kind using property checks or workarounds. This function provides a single-point, reliable check for invariance.
Ignore potential default or uninitialized dates in business logic. Unspecified dates can introduce subtle bugs if left unchecked.

elements.title: IsNumeric
elements.summary: Determines whether a string is a valid numeric value, with optional support for hexadecimal input.

exceptions.trigger: sNumber is NIL or not a string.

caveats: NaN, positive infinity, and negative infinity return .F..
Hexadecimal support is limited to whole values in 0x... or 0X... form.

do: Use IsNumeric before ToNumeric when invalid input should be handled without exceptions.
Pass bAllowHex as .T. only when 0x-prefixed values are valid for that input.
Keep user input as a string until validation is complete.
dont: Assume NIL or non-string values return .F.. They raise an error instead.
Assume hexadecimal strings are accepted by default. They only pass when bAllowHex is .T..
Use IsNumeric when you need the parsed numeric value. Use ToNumeric or Val after validation.

elements.title: IsProductionModeOn
elements.summary: Returns .T. when the application's production mode flag is enabled and .F. otherwise.
caveats: Returns only production vs. non-production — it does not indicate which specific non-production environment is active (e.g., development, staging).
Changes to the production mode status require a configuration update and restart; the result does not reflect real-time changes during a session.
This function does not validate license types or features.

do: Use this function to clearly separate code paths that should only run in production environments.
Combine this function with logging or error-handling logic for environments where stricter compliance is necessary.
dont: Rely on it for detailed environment distinctions beyond production vs. non-production. The function provides only a binary .T./.F. value and does not distinguish between various non-production modes.
Use it as a substitute for user authentication or license validation logic. Production mode only reflects the application environment, not user rights or licenses.

elements.title: IsTable
elements.summary: Checks whether a table exists in a database connection.

exceptions.message: The table name parameter is null

exceptions.trigger: sConnectionName or sTableName is an empty string.

caveats: The function reports only whether the table exists; it does not return schema details.
The function checks one table name at a time.

do: Check the result before running queries or updates that assume a table exists.
Pass the exact connection name and table name used in your environment.
Use NIL for sConnectionName only when you intentionally want the default connection.
dont: Pass NIL for sTableName; this raises an immediate error.
Treat an empty string as the same as omitting sConnectionName; empty strings raise an error instead of falling back.
Use IsTable as a substitute for retrieving column details; use a schema-inspection function when you need more than existence.

elements.title: IsTableFld
elements.summary: Checks whether a field exists in a table for a selected database connection.

exceptions.message: The table name or field name parameter is null

exceptions.trigger: sConnectionName, sTableName, or sFieldName is an empty string.

caveats: The function checks one table and one field name at a time.
It reports existence only; it does not return field metadata.
If the selected connection cannot be opened, the call can fail for reasons unrelated to field existence.

do: Check the result before generating SQL, importing data, or reading optional fields.
Use NIL for sConnectionName only when you intentionally want the default connection.
Use IsTable for table-only checks and TableFldLst when you need all field names.
dont: Assume invalid input returns .F.. NIL and empty-string cases can raise errors instead.
Treat "" as the same as omitting sConnectionName. Only NIL falls back to the default connection.
Pass an empty sFieldName expecting a table-only check. Use IsTable for that case.

elements.title: JDay
elements.summary: Returns the day-of-year number for a date, or for today's date when no argument is supplied.

exceptions.trigger: dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: Argument: dDate must be of date type

caveats: The empty-date fallback is 34 (February 3rd), which overlaps with a real day-of-year value. Check for empty dates before calling JDay() when that distinction matters.
The normal return range is 1 through 366; 366 occurs only in leap years.

do: Validate or document how your code handles empty dates before relying on a JDay() result.
Use JDay() when you intentionally want the current date by omitting the argument.
Use DOY instead when empty dates should raise rather than returning a sentinel value.
dont: Pass strings, numbers, or objects expecting automatic conversion to a date.
Treat 34 as a guaranteed real day-of-year result when the input may be empty.
Use JDay() to calculate the number of days between two dates.

elements.title: LCase
elements.summary: Conditionally evaluates one of two SSL expressions supplied as strings.
caveats: LCase can return different value types depending on the selected expression.
Only the selected branch is executed.
Whitespace-only sFalseValue is treated the same as an empty false branch and returns "".
If the selected expression is not valid in the current context, the evaluation can fail.

do: Pass valid SSL expressions as strings, such as variable names, function calls, or quoted literals.
Use quoted string literals inside the expression text when you want a string result, for example LCase(bFlag, '"Yes"', '"No"').
Keep branch expressions short and predictable so the selected result is easy to understand.
dont: Treat sTrueValue and sFalseValue as plain return text. LCase executes them as SSL.
Pass unquoted text when you mean a string literal. LCase(bFlag, "Yes", "No") tries to evaluate Yes or No as identifiers, not string literals.
Use untrusted input as expression text. The selected branch is executed.

elements.title: LDAPAuth
elements.summary: Authenticates a user by binding directly to an LDAP server.

exceptions.message: The parameters are not correct.(sLdapHost or sLdapUserName are null)

exceptions.trigger: The LDAP bind fails with an LDAP error.
exceptions.message: Error occurred :\nBinding operation for user : {sLdapUserName} failed with error : \n{message}

exceptions.trigger: The LDAP bind fails with a general error.
exceptions.message: Exception :\nBinding operation for user : {sLdapUserName} failed with error : \n{message}

caveats: This function performs a direct bind only. It does not search LDAP to discover a user DN first.
sLdapDistinctiveName is only checked for the value "ntlm" in a case-insensitive comparison.
The function always disposes the LDAP connection before returning or propagating an exception.

do: Validate sLdapHost, sLdapUserName, and sLdapPassword before calling the function so failures are easier to explain to users.
Use bSecure with the correct LDAPS port when the directory requires encrypted binds.
Treat an empty return value as success and handle failures in :CATCH by reading GetLastSSLError():Description.
dont: Treat the return value as a boolean or status code. This function succeeds with "" and raises exceptions on errors.
Omit sLdapPassword for a normal bind. An empty password triggers The password cannot be null.
Pass sLdapDistinctiveName unless you intentionally need the NTLM mode switch. Other behavior is not implemented here.

elements.title: LDAPAuthEX
elements.summary: Authenticates an LDAP user by searching for exactly one directory entry and then binding as that user.

exceptions.message: The parameters are not correct.(sLdapHost or sBindUserName are null)

exceptions.trigger: The bind operation fails with an LDAP error.
exceptions.message: Error occurred :\nBinding operation for user : {sBindUserName} failed with error : \n{message}

exceptions.trigger: The bind operation fails with a general error.
exceptions.message: Exception :\nBinding operation for user : {sBindUserName} failed with error : \n{message}
Error : searching for: {sSearchUserName}; start search location: {sLdapDistinguishedNameStartSearch} filter: {sSearchFilter}\nreturned an error: \n{message}

exceptions.trigger: The search returns zero or multiple entries.
exceptions.message: The search returned : {count} entries for the search filter : {formattedFilter}

exceptions.trigger: The matched entry's DN cannot be read.
exceptions.message: Cannot extract the DN from the search response for the search filter : {sSearchFilter}

caveats: If sSearchFilter is omitted, the function uses (&(objectClass=user)(name={0})).
Custom filters are passed through string.Format(sSearchFilter, sSearchUserName), so filters that use {0} are populated with sSearchUserName before the LDAP search runs.
The search must return exactly one entry. Zero matches and multi-match results both raise exceptions.
When one of the requested attributes is missing, its slot in the returned comma-delimited string is empty.
When an attribute value itself contains commas, the returned multi-attribute string is ambiguous because values are joined without escaping.
sLdapDistinguishedName is present in the signature but has no effect.

do: Use a search filter that resolves to exactly one entry for the supplied sSearchUserName.
Use .T. for bSecure and the appropriate LDAP SSL port when credentials must be protected in transit.
Omit sAuthAttribName when you only need an authentication check and no attribute value.
Request only the attributes you actually need, and keep their order stable if downstream code depends on the returned comma-delimited string.
dont: Rely on broad filters that can return more than one entry. The function raises unless the search finds exactly one match.
Pass empty passwords and expect the function to report a simple false result. Bind failures raise exceptions.
Assume sLdapDistinguishedName changes the search or bind behavior. It has no effect.
Request comma-containing attribute values if you need lossless multi-value parsing from the returned string. Multiple requested attributes are joined with literal commas and are not escaped.

elements.title: LDir
elements.summary: Retrieves an array of file and directory names matching a specified pattern and optional attribute filter.

exceptions.trigger: The specified path is not in the allowed folders.

caveats: LDir returns only names. If you need paths or metadata, call Directory instead.
An empty result means either no entries matched or the lookup failed for a normal filesystem reason such as a missing directory.
Unsupported attribute characters are ignored.
D, H, and S include additional entry types; they do not remove regular visible files from the result.

do: Supply a fully qualified directory or directory-plus-pattern such as C:\\reports\\*.pdf so the lookup target is explicit.
Use D, H, and S only when you intentionally want to include directories, hidden entries, or system entries.
Handle empty arrays defensively, as no matches found is not an error.
Use Directory when you need size, date, time, or attribute metadata instead of just names.
dont: Omit sFilePattern or pass NIL expecting a safe default. LDir reaches DosSupport, which treats a missing first argument as an error.
Treat D, H, or S as exclusive filters such as "directories only" or "hidden only". They add those entry types to the normal visible-file results.
Write downstream logic that assumes LDir always returns results. Filters or missing files yield empty arrays and must be handled gracefully.
Try to derive file size, timestamps, attributes, or full paths from LDir results. The function only returns names.

elements.title: LFromHex
elements.summary: Converts a hexadecimal string to a string by reading the input in two-character chunks and decoding each chunk as a byte.

do: Validate external input with IsHex before calling LFromHex.
Use LToHex and LFromHex together when you need a simple round-trip conversion.
Treat NIL as an error case and handle it before decoding.
dont: Assume external input is valid hexadecimal text. Invalid content can fail during decoding.
Pass NIL to LFromHex. The function raises an exception for NIL input.
Use LFromHex when you need a numeric conversion. Use LHex2Dec for hexadecimal-to-decimal conversion.

elements.title: LHex2Dec
elements.summary: Converts a hexadecimal string to its decimal string representation.

do: Validate external input before calling LHex2Dec().
Normalize incoming values first when your input may contain surrounding whitespace or mixed casing.
Use IsHex when you want to reject non-hex content before conversion.
dont: Assume invalid input is corrected automatically. Bad input still fails during conversion.
Pass NIL to LHex2Dec(). The function raises an error for NIL input.
Use LHex2Dec() when you need byte-to-text decoding. Use LFromHex for that.

elements.title: LIMSDate
elements.summary: Returns a date value as a formatted string.

exceptions.trigger: vDate is neither a string nor a date.
exceptions.message: Argument vDate must be a date or string value.

caveats: String input is parsed with the current application date format, not an explicitly supplied parse format.

do: Pass an SSL date when you already have one and only use string input when parsing is intentional.
Specify the output format explicitly when downstream code depends on a fixed date shape.
Handle null-date placeholder output when your source data may contain empty or invalid dates.
dont: Pass arrays, objects, or other non-date values. Those inputs raise an error.
Assume an omitted format uses the current global date format. LIMSDate defaults to dd-MMM-yy instead.
Assume a format containing MMM preserves your exact token pattern. LIMSDate normalizes those cases to dd-MMM-yyyy.

elements.title: LKill
elements.summary: Deletes a public variable from the current SSL session by name and returns an empty string.
caveats: Removing a public variable can change later code paths that expect the shared value to still exist.

do: Use LKill() for cleanup of public variables that were created dynamically for the current session.
Keep the public-variable name in a string variable when the name is determined at runtime.
Use IsDefined if later logic needs to confirm the public variable is gone.
dont: Use LKill() as a substitute for clearing a local variable. It only removes public variables.
Assume a missing public variable is an error condition. LKill() simply does nothing when the name is not present.
Rely on undocumented input handling for invalid arguments. Pass a valid public-variable name string.

elements.title: LLower
elements.summary: Converts all characters in a string to their lowercase equivalents.
caveats: Case conversion depends on the current culture, so some characters can lowercase differently in different locales.

do: Normalize both values before an exact string comparison when case should not matter.
Combine LLower with AllTrim when input may contain extra spaces.
Use Lower and LLower interchangeably — both produce identical output.
dont: Assume LLower handles NIL input silently. It raises an error instead.
Expect LLower to trim or remove whitespace. It changes case only.
Compare unnormalized mixed-case strings directly when the match should be case-insensitive.

elements.title: LSearch
elements.summary: Returns a single value from a SQL query, or a caller-supplied fallback when the query produces no scalar result.

exceptions.trigger: The connection cannot resolve a database engine.
exceptions.message: Cannot determine the database engine name.

exceptions.trigger: sConnectionName does not identify a configured connection.

exceptions.message: The current array has more than 1 dimension.

exceptions.trigger: The number of ? placeholders does not match the number of supplied values.

caveats: LSearch falls back only when the scalar result is missing or database NULL. An actual empty string from the database is returned as-is.

do: Pass an explicit fallback that matches how your script should behave when no row is found.
Use positional ? placeholders with aArrayOfValues instead of concatenating values into SQL text.
Use NIL as the fallback when you need to distinguish missing data from an empty string or zero.
Switch to LSelect, LSelect1, or LSelectC when you need rows rather than one scalar value.
dont: Use ?varName? syntax with LSearch. That substitution style is for SQLExecute only.
Treat LSearch as a multi-row query helper. It is meant for a single scalar result.
Pass a multidimensional array to aArrayOfValues; it raises an error before execution.
Rely on an implicit fallback. Decide whether your missing-data case should return "", 0, NIL, .F., or another explicit value.

elements.title: LSelect
elements.summary: Executes a SQL SELECT statement and returns the result as a two-dimensional SSL array.

exceptions.trigger: sConnectionName does not match a configured connection.
The connection cannot resolve a database engine.
exceptions.message: Cannot determine the database engine name.
The current array has more than 1 dimension.

exceptions.trigger: The number of ? placeholders does not match the number of supplied values.

caveats: LSelect currently behaves like LSelect1 because the aFieldList path is not implemented.
If you need to skip aFieldList and still pass later arguments, keep the skipped-argument commas adjacent, for example LSelect(sSQL,,"DATABASE",{sID});.
aInvariantDateCols is interpreted either as names or as numeric indexes. Use one style consistently within the same array.

do: Skip aFieldList entirely. The current LSelect implementation does not support it.
Use positional ? placeholders with aArrayOfValues instead of concatenating values into SQL text.
Choose bNullAsBlank deliberately so your script handles missing database values the way you expect.
Use aInvariantDateCols when returned date columns must not be shifted by timezone conversion.
dont: Pass {} or any other value for aFieldList; LSelect raises an error instead of filtering columns.
Use ?varName? syntax with LSelect. That style is for SQLExecute, not LSelect.
Pass a multidimensional array in aArrayOfValues; query preparation fails before execution.
Assume bNullAsBlank means every NULL becomes an empty string. Numeric, logic, and date columns surface their own empty default values.

elements.title: LSelect1
elements.summary: Executes a parameterized SQL SELECT command and returns the result as an array of rows.

exceptions.message: The current array has more than 1 dimension.

exceptions.trigger: sConnectionName does not match a configured connection.
The connection cannot resolve a database engine.
exceptions.message: Cannot determine the database engine name.

exceptions.trigger: The number of ? placeholders does not match the number of supplied values.

caveats: A database connection is closed after the reader is consumed, even when the query raises an error.

do: Check ALen(aRows) before processing the result.
Use ? placeholders with aArrayOfValues instead of concatenating values into SQL.
Pass bNullAsBlank explicitly when downstream logic depends on how database NULL values are surfaced.
Use aInvariantDateCols for date columns that must keep their stored value without timezone adjustment.
dont: Use ?varName? syntax with LSelect1. That syntax is for SQLExecute, not LSelect1.
Assume a successful call returned data. An empty result is still a valid array.
Pass a multidimensional values array.
Use invariant date handling unless the date column really needs to bypass timezone conversion.

elements.title: LSelectC
elements.summary: Executes a SQL SELECT statement and returns the result as a two-dimensional SSL array.

exceptions.trigger: sConnectionName does not match a configured connection.
The connection cannot resolve a database engine.
exceptions.message: Cannot determine the database engine name.
The current array has more than 1 dimension.

exceptions.trigger: The number of ? placeholders does not match the number of supplied values.

caveats: LSelectC currently behaves the same as LSelect.
If you need to skip aFieldList and still pass later arguments, keep the skipped-argument commas adjacent, for example LSelectC(sSQL,, "DATABASE", {sID});.
aInvariantDateCols is interpreted either as names or as numeric indexes. Use one style consistently within the same array.

do: Skip aFieldList entirely. The current LSelectC implementation does not support it.
Use positional ? placeholders with aArrayOfValues instead of concatenating values into SQL text.
Choose bNullAsBlank deliberately so your script handles missing database values the way you expect.
Use aInvariantDateCols when returned date columns must not be shifted by timezone conversion.
dont: Pass {} or any other value for aFieldList; LSelectC raises an error instead of filtering columns.
Use ?varName? syntax with LSelectC. That style is for SQLExecute, not LSelectC.
Pass a multidimensional array in aArrayOfValues; query preparation fails before execution.
Assume bNullAsBlank means every NULL becomes an empty string. Numeric, logic, and date columns surface their own empty default values.

elements.title: LStr
elements.summary: Converts a value to its trimmed string representation, returning \"NIL\" when the input is NIL.
caveats: NIL input returns the four-character string "NIL", not an empty string — add a guard when a blank placeholder is expected instead.
Leading and trailing whitespace in the converted result is trimmed; strings with intentional leading or trailing spaces will lose them.
Passing complex types such as objects returns their default string representation, which may not match expectations.

do: Use LStr when NIL values must appear as the explicit placeholder "NIL" in UI output, logs, or exports.
Pair LStr with LTransform or StrZero when field-width padding or decimal precision is required after conversion.
Apply LStr when serializing mixed data sources that must never produce empty strings for missing values.
dont: Assume the output will be empty or NIL; LStr always returns a non-empty string.
Use LStr when strict output formatting or padding is required — use LTransform or StrZero instead.
Use the result directly in numeric operations; the output is always a string and must be reconverted if a number is needed.

elements.title: LToHex
elements.summary: Converts a string or integer to hexadecimal text.

exceptions.trigger: vSource is a non-empty unsupported type, a non-integer numeric value, or outside the supported numeric range.
exceptions.message: Argument: vSource must be a string or an integer.

caveats: Numeric 0 is considered empty by Empty and is returned unchanged rather than converted to "00000000".
String conversion is byte-oriented, so the output is best treated as encoded data rather than display text.
For string input, byte values below 16 are space-padded by the formatter rather than zero-padded.

do: Pass strings when you need a text-to-hex conversion.
Pass only whole-number numeric values when you need the 8-character integer form.
Use LFromHex to verify round-trip string conversions.
dont: Assume every numeric value is accepted. Decimal values and out-of-range integers raise an error.
Assume empty values are converted. LToHex returns them unchanged.
Use LToHex when you need hexadecimal-to-decimal conversion. Use LHex2Dec for that direction.

elements.title: LTransform
elements.summary: Formats a numeric expression as a string by applying a picture string.

do: Use LTransform when you need a specific numeric display mask instead of a generic conversion.
Choose a picture string that matches the width and decimal precision you actually need in the output.
Handle mixed-type input deliberately when the picture should apply only to numeric values.
dont: Assume # and 9 behave differently in LTransform. Both are converted to 0 before formatting.
Expect sPicture to affect strings, dates, or other non-numeric values. Non-numeric input is returned as plain text.
Assume a NIL input will be preserved as "NIL" or raise an error. LTransform returns an empty string instead.

elements.title: LTrim
elements.summary: Removes leading whitespace from a string.
caveats: Tabs, line breaks, and other leading whitespace characters are trimmed along with spaces.

do: Use LTrim when only the left side of the string should change.
Check the result with Empty after trimming when the input may be blank or whitespace-only.
Use AllTrim instead when you need to remove padding from both ends.
dont: Use LTrim when trailing whitespace also matters — it only trims the start of the string.
Assume whitespace-only input will produce a non-empty string safe to concatenate without checking first.
Pass NIL expecting a silent empty result — the function raises an error.

elements.title: LWait
elements.summary: Blocks further script execution for a specified number of seconds and returns an empty string.
caveats: Passing a negative number raises an error; clamp inputs to zero or above before calling. NIL and omitted arguments are treated as zero and do not raise.
Fractional seconds are truncated to whole seconds; LWait(0.9) is the same as LWait(0) and introduces no delay.
Calling LWait in user-interactive or UI-sensitive code may freeze the interface during the wait.

do: Use for explicit pauses between actions in automated, non-user-interactive code.
Clamp nSeconds to zero or above before calling when the value may be negative.
Capture the result only if needed; the return value is always "".
dont: Use LWait in code paths that require UI responsiveness or asynchronous activities.
Add LWait to temporarily work around race conditions or timing bugs in production scripts. Relying on fixed delays leads to fragile and unreliable automation.
Pass non-numeric types as nSeconds. Unrecognized types default to zero, causing the wait to be silently skipped.

elements.title: Left
elements.summary: Extracts the leftmost characters from a string.
caveats: An empty sSource with a positive nLength returns an empty string.
nLength is rounded before evaluation, so decimal values can change whether the result is a string or NIL.
Left does not pad results — if fewer characters are available, it returns only the available characters.

do: Use Left when you need a clear, direct prefix extraction.
Check for NIL when nLength may evaluate to zero or less.
Normalize or validate the requested length before appending extra text such as ellipses.
dont: Assume zero or negative lengths return an empty string — Left returns NIL for those cases.
Recreate prefix logic with SubStr or manual index math when Left expresses the intent directly.
Ignore rounding behavior when passing decimal lengths — the function rounds before extracting characters.

elements.title: Len
elements.summary: Returns the number of characters in a string or the element count of an array.

exceptions.trigger: vSource is neither a string nor an array.

caveats: For arrays, only top-level elements are counted — nested arrays are not expanded and their contents are not included in the count.

do: Check for NIL before passing a value when the source may be absent.
Use Len for string length and ALen when the input is always an array.
Treat a zero result as empty and validate further when needed.
dont: Assume NIL is handled gracefully — it raises an error.
Interpret a length of zero as valid content without further checks.
Substitute Len for ALen when the input is always an array — prefer ALen to make the intent explicit.

elements.title: LimsAt
elements.summary: Finds the first occurrence of a substring in a string at or after a 1-based starting position.
caveats: The result is 1-based, so a return value of 1 is a valid match at the first character.

do: Test the result against 0 before using it as a position.
Pass nPos + 1 when you want to continue searching after an earlier match.
Use LimsAt when you need the first match after a known position.
Validate inputs that may be NIL before calling the function.
dont: Check for -1 — LimsAt returns 0 when no match is found.
Pass a 0-based offset copied from another language without converting it.
Expect NIL arguments to return 0 or NIL — they raise an error.
Use LimsAt when you actually need the last occurrence or pattern-based search.

elements.title: LimsExec
elements.summary: Launches an external application without waiting for it to finish.
caveats: Only a NIL sApplication raises an exception — empty strings and invalid paths return .F. and set the last SSL error.
Startup failures return .F., set the last SSL error, and write the error message.
When bShow is .F., the function requests a hidden window, but the final behavior still depends on the launched application.

do: Use sArguments for normal argument passing so the executable path stays clear and easy to validate.
Check the boolean result and inspect GetLastSSLError when the launch returns .F..
Use RunApp instead when the workflow must wait for the external program to finish.
dont: Assume .T. means the external program completed successfully. It only means the process was started.
Pass an empty, malformed, or unquoted executable path. Invalid sApplication values raise exceptions instead of returning .F..
Ignore paths with spaces when embedding the full command line in sApplication. Quote the executable path first so SSL can split the command correctly.

elements.title: LimsGetDateFormat
elements.summary: Returns the current global date format string used for date parsing and formatting.
caveats: Changing the global date format via DateFormat does not notify all clients in real time; always call this function each time you need the value.
The function returns the format string but does not validate it; a misconfigured format string may cause downstream parsing errors.

do: Call LimsGetDateFormat() at runtime before parsing or formatting dates rather than hard-coding a format string.
Use the returned format string when parsing user input or data files.
Document the expected date format for integration partners and when writing export/import routines.
dont: Hard-code date format strings directly in your logic.
Assume a specific date order or delimiter based on regional habits.
Ignore the global configuration when presenting or persisting date strings.

elements.title: LimsNETCast
elements.summary: Prepares a value for a requested interop type such as an enum, by-reference value, numeric type, or typed array.

exceptions.trigger: sNewType starts with enum: and vVal is not a string or number.
exceptions.message: Argument vVal must be a number or a string.

exceptions.trigger: vVal is numeric and sNewType is not a valid numeric target type.

caveats: byref is matched case-insensitively.
If vVal is neither an enum candidate, a numeric value, nor an array, the function returns the original value unless sNewType is byref.
Array casts may still fail later if the array contents do not fit the requested target array type.

do: Validate sNewType before calling so the cast path is explicit.
Use enum: only with a string or numeric source value.
Use specific numeric and array target types when an interop API expects them.
dont: Pass unchecked user input directly into sNewType — invalid type strings fail at runtime.
Use enum casts with arrays, objects, or other unsupported source types.
Assume every type name forces a conversion; unsupported combinations return the original value unchanged.

elements.title: LimsNETConnect
elements.summary: Loads a .NET assembly, resolves a type, and either returns a type handle or creates an instance for SSL interop.

exceptions.trigger: The requested type cannot be resolved.

do: Pass bAsStatic as .T. only when you want the type itself for static member access.
Pass constructor arguments as an array, even for a single argument.
Use fully qualified type names so resolution is predictable.
Wrap calls in :TRY / :CATCH when the assembly, type, or constructor may fail at runtime.
dont: Pass non-string values for sAssembly or sTypeName, or a non-array value for aArgs.
Omit sTypeName unless you actually want the assembly object rather than a type or instance.
Pass a single constructor argument directly. Use {value} rather than value.
Assume a .dll name will resolve unless that file exists at the provided path or in the application's Components folder.

elements.title: LimsNETTypeOf
elements.summary: Resolves a .NET type name string to a .NET Type object.

exceptions.trigger: sTypeName is not a string or trims to an empty value.
exceptions.message: Argument must be a non-empty string.

exceptions.trigger: The type name cannot be resolved.

caveats: A whitespace-only string is invalid.
The not-found message uses the resolved full name when an alias expands first, such as System.Int32 for int.

do: Validate dynamic input before calling the function.
Use fully qualified names for custom types or when alias resolution is not enough.
Wrap user-driven or configuration-driven lookups in :TRY / :CATCH.
dont: Pass numbers, objects, or arrays and expect implicit conversion to a type name.
Assume every short name will resolve outside the built-in aliases.
Continue as if lookup always succeeds when the type name comes from external input.

elements.title: LimsOleConnect
elements.summary: Creates an object from a ProgID so SSL code can work with an OLE or COM automation server.

exceptions.trigger: The ProgID cannot be resolved or the object cannot be created.

caveats: Available members depend entirely on the object created by that ProgID.
Cleanup is not automatic; use EndLimsOleConnect explicitly when you finish with the object.
The ProgID StarLIMS.SoapClient.SoapClient is handled specially and returns the platform SOAP client object instead of creating an external COM server.

do: Pass a known, valid ProgID.
Release the object with EndLimsOleConnect when you are done.
Wrap the call in :TRY / :CATCH when the ProgID or the target application may be unavailable.
dont: Assume every ProgID is registered on every machine.
Skip cleanup after creating automation objects.
Document or code against members unless the target automation server actually provides them.

elements.title: LimsRecordsAffected
elements.summary: Returns the number of records affected by the most recent database operation.
caveats: If the database connection changes context between operations, the result may not match the previous transaction.

do: Call immediately after the database operation you want to measure.
Use to verify critical batch processes by comparing expected and actual affected records.
Use in error handling routines to detect unintended changes.
dont: Rely on its value after running other queries. The count reflects only the most recent modification statement.
Assume all operations that modify data update this count reliably. Some drivers or non-standard SQL operations may behave differently.
Trust the count if there was an error or transaction rollback. Failed or rolled back operations reset or leave the count at 0.

elements.title: LimsSetCounter
elements.summary: Generates the next counter value and, when given matching field and value arrays, inserts a new row with that key.
caveats: If aFields and aValues have different lengths, the counter value is still generated, but the row insert is skipped.
nIncrementWith affects how far the stored counter advances, but the returned numeric value is still the first value from that reserved range.
Insert-failure handling is not identical in every deployment. Do not assume the generated number was rolled back unless you have verified that behavior in your environment.

do: Pass only additional insert columns in aFields; sFieldName is inserted automatically.
Keep aFields and aValues aligned by position and length.
Check for a 0 return when the counter cannot be generated.
Wrap calls in :TRY / :CATCH when the inputs or database state may be unreliable.
dont: Put sFieldName in aFields; that produces a duplicate insert column.
Assume empty strings are treated the same as NIL; only NIL table or field arguments short-circuit to 0 before work starts.
Assume mismatched arrays will still insert a partial row; the function skips the insert when the array lengths differ.
Assume an insert failure always means the generated number can be reused; some environments may still consume the next counter value.

elements.title: LimsSqlConnect
elements.summary: Registers a configured database connection by connection name.

exceptions.trigger: The connection registration step throws an internal error.

caveats: If sConnectionName does not match a configured connection name, the function returns .F..
If the name is tenant-specific and the current tenant context is missing or does not match the tenant encoded in the name, the function returns .F..
If the runtime has not initialized the active connection collection, the function returns .F..
Re-registering an existing connection name replaces the currently registered entry for that name.

do: Validate that the connection name exists in configuration before calling the function.
Check the boolean return value before running SQL against that connection name.
Disconnect with LimsSqlDisconnect when the named connection is no longer needed.
dont: Assume LimsSqlConnect changes the default connection. It only registers the named connection.
Treat .F. as an exception-only case. Unknown names and unavailable tenant-specific names can fail by returning .F..
Re-register the same connection name without a reason. A later registration replaces the existing named entry.

elements.title: LimsSqlDisconnect
elements.summary: Closes an active database connection by name and removes it from the internal registry.
caveats: Calling with NIL, an empty string, or a misspelled name will not disconnect any connection and returns .F..
Disconnecting a connection that is in use by other operations can cause those operations to fail.

do: Check the boolean return value to confirm the disconnect succeeded.
Disconnect all named database connections during application shutdown or cleanup routines.
Use :FINALLY to guarantee disconnection regardless of whether the block succeeds or raises an error.
dont: Assume success without checking the return value.
Leave connections open unnecessarily.
Silently ignore a .F. return; it can indicate the connection was never registered or was already closed.

elements.title: LimsString
elements.summary: Converts a value to a string, returning \"NIL\" when the input is NIL.

do: Use LimsString before concatenating numbers or optional values into messages.
Expect "NIL" when the source value is missing.
Use it when numeric text should follow the current decimal and group separator settings.
dont: Assume a missing value becomes an empty string. LimsString(NIL) returns "NIL".
Assume numeric output is culture-neutral. It follows the current separator settings.
Use LimsString when you need a custom numeric mask. Use a formatting function such as LTransform instead.

elements.title: LimsTime
elements.summary: Returns the current time as a formatted string.
caveats: If the configured time format changes, the returned string changes with it.

do: Use LimsTime() when you need a display-ready time string.
Capture the value once and reuse it when several messages should show the same time within one operation.
Pair it with Today() or Now() when your logic also needs date context.
dont: Use LimsTime() when you need a date-time value for comparisons or calculations.
Assume the returned string always uses one fixed format.
Call LimsTime() repeatedly if a single action should keep one consistent displayed time.

elements.title: LimsType
elements.summary: Returns the single-character SSL type code for a variable name or expression.

exceptions.message: The argument for function LimsType must be a string.

caveats: Evaluating expressions may have side effects if the expression calls functions or modifies state.
The function executes code to resolve the value; avoid passing untrusted input.

do: Pass a variable name as a string (e.g. LimsType("sMyVar")) when you want the type of a local variable.
Check for "U", "UI", and "UE" explicitly — they are normal return values, not exceptions.
Use LimsTypeEx instead when you already have the value and do not need to reference it by name.
dont: Pass the value itself rather than a string. LimsType requires a string argument.
Assume evaluation of an expression is side-effect-free. The function executes the expression to obtain a result.
Pass non-string types (numbers, arrays, objects) as the argument — they raise an error.

elements.title: LimsTypeEx
elements.summary: Returns the public SSL type name for a value.

do: Compare the return value against the documented uppercase names such as STRING, NUMERIC, and NIL.
Use LimsTypeEx before type-specific operations when input may vary at runtime.
Handle NIL explicitly when blank or missing input is meaningful to your logic.
dont: Assume every incoming value is already the type you expect.
Infer a value's type from how it prints or from user-facing formatting.
Treat LimsTypeEx as a validator for business rules; it reports type, not whether the value is acceptable for your process.

elements.title: LimsXOr
elements.summary: Calculates the bitwise exclusive OR of two integer-valued numbers.

exceptions.trigger: nVal1 or nVal2 is NIL. The runtime raises the same message regardless of which argument is NIL.
nVal1 or nVal2 has a fractional part.
exceptions.message: Xor is available only for integer numbers.

do: Use LimsXOr with integer flags and masks.
Validate or normalize values before the call when inputs may contain decimals.
Use XOR's reversible behavior when you need to toggle or restore integer bits.
dont: Pass fractional numeric values such as 7.5. The function only accepts integer-valued numbers.
Assume a missing argument is treated as zero. NIL raises an error.
Use LimsXOr for logical true-or-false comparisons. It is a numeric bitwise operation.

elements.title: Lower
elements.summary: Converts all characters in a string to lowercase.
caveats: Case conversion depends on the current culture, so some characters can lowercase differently in different locales.

do: Normalize both values before an exact string comparison when case should not matter.
Combine Lower with AllTrim when input may contain extra spaces.
Use Lower and LLower interchangeably only when you want the same behavior.
dont: Assume Lower handles NIL input silently. It raises an error instead.
Expect Lower to trim or remove whitespace. It changes case only.
Compare mixed-case strings directly when the match should be case-insensitive.

elements.title: MakeDateInvariant
elements.summary: Marks a date, or selected date columns in an array, as invariant.

exceptions.trigger: vDate is not a date or array.
exceptions.message: Argument: vDate is neither a Date nor an Array.

exceptions.trigger: vDate is an array and vColumnsIndex is NIL or omitted.
vColumnsIndex is not a number or array.
exceptions.message: Argument: vColumnsIndex is neither a Number nor an Array.

exceptions.trigger: A selected position in vDate does not contain a date.

caveats: For array input, missing positions are skipped, but existing selected values must be dates.

do: Use MakeDateInvariant(vDate) for a single date and omit vColumnsIndex.
Pass explicit column numbers when converting dates inside arrays or rowsets.
Pair it with IsInvariantDate when you need to verify the result.
dont: Pass strings, numbers, or mixed-type target columns. The function raises an error when a selected value is not a date.
Omit vColumnsIndex when vDate is an array. Array input requires a column number or an array of column numbers.
Treat this as a time-zone conversion. It changes the date kind only; it does not adjust the stored date or time.

elements.title: MakeDateLocal
elements.summary: Sets a date value, or selected date columns in an array, to local date kind in place.

exceptions.trigger: vDate is not a date or array.
exceptions.message: Argument: vDate is neither a Date nor an Array.

exceptions.trigger: vDate is an array and vColumnsIndex is NIL or omitted.
vColumnsIndex is not a number or array.
exceptions.message: Argument: vColumnsIndex is neither a Number nor an Array.

exceptions.trigger: A targeted column in vDate does not contain a date.

caveats: If a targeted column position is missing in a row, the function skips it.

do: Pass vColumnsIndex only when the first argument is an array.
Validate that every targeted column contains dates before updating an array in place.
Use MakeDateInvariant when you need the date kind to remain invariant instead of local.
dont: Assume the function creates a new date or array. It mutates the value you pass in.
Pass text, numbers, or mixed columns as date targets.
Pass vColumnsIndex when working with a single date and expect it to affect behavior.

elements.title: MakeDirOnFtp
elements.summary: Creates a remote directory by using FTP, or by using SFTP when bIsSFTP is .T..

exceptions.trigger: sServerNameOrIP is NIL or empty (FTP path).
exceptions.message: FTP server name or IP cannot be missing.

exceptions.trigger: sServerNameOrIP is NIL or empty (SFTP path).

elements.title: Min

exceptions.trigger: vValue1 and vValue2 are different types.

elements.title: Minute

exceptions.message: Argument: dDate must be of date type

exceptions.trigger: dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: Argument: dDate must be of date type

elements.title: NoOfDays

exceptions.trigger: dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: Argument: dDate must be of date type

do: Use NoOfDays() instead of hard-coding month lengths.
Treat a 0 result as an empty-date case and handle it before using the value in downstream logic.

dont: Pass NIL or non-date values. NoOfDays() validates the argument and raises on invalid input.
Assume every month has 30 or 31 days, or that February is always 28.

elements.title: Nothing

do: Use Nothing() when your input rules explicitly treat "0" as missing data.
Use Nothing() on mixed-type values when NIL, blank input, 0, and .F. should all stop processing.

dont: Use Nothing() when the literal string "0" is a valid code, quantity, or response in your workflow.

elements.title: Now

Expect sItemType to affect populated arrays. Typed sentinels are only
