bIncludeHeader is provided but is not a boolean.

caveats: Returning large result sets as arrays, XML, or dataset objects can increase memory use.

do: Use named ?varName? placeholders instead of building SQL by concatenating values into the string.
Set vReturnType explicitly when downstream code depends on XML or dataset output.
Check the boolean return value for non-SELECT statements, especially when transaction behavior matters.
dont: Use ?varName? syntax with RunSQL, LSearch, LSelect, LSelect1, LSelectC, or GetDataSet; those functions use positional ? placeholders with an explicit values array.
Assume every string vReturnType changes the output shape; only "xml" and "dataset" have special handling.
Concatenate user or runtime values directly into SQL text when SQLExecute can bind them for you.

elements.title: SQLRemoveComments
elements.summary: Removes SQL comments from a string and returns the cleaned SQL text.

exceptions.trigger: sStatement is not a string and not NIL.

caveats: -- comments are removed through the end of the line.
The function does not check whether the remaining SQL is valid.

do: Use SQLRemoveComments when you need cleaned SQL text for logging,
Handle the NIL return path when the source SQL text can be missing.
Keep optimizer hints in mind: /*+ ... */ is preserved rather than removed.
dont: Treat this as SQL validation or SQL injection protection; it only
Assume every /* ... */ sequence will be stripped; optimizer hints
Pass numbers, arrays, objects, or booleans; non-string non-NIL

elements.title: Scient
elements.summary: Converts a number to its scientific notation string representation.

do: Treat the result as formatted text, not as a numeric value for further math.
Use this function when the built-in Scient output is acceptable as-is.
Account for zero and negative inputs if those values matter to the output you show or export.
dont: Assume the return value can be used directly in numeric calculations without converting it back.
Assume the mantissa always uses . as the decimal separator.
Use Scient when you need caller-controlled precision. Use ToScientific for that case.

elements.title: SearchLDAPUser
elements.summary: Searches LDAP for exactly one user and returns that entry's distinguished name.

exceptions.message: The parameters are not correct.(sLdapHost or sBindUserName are null)

exceptions.trigger: The bind operation fails with an LDAP error.
exceptions.message: Error occurred :\nBinding operation for user : {sBindUserName} failed with error : \n{message}

exceptions.trigger: The bind operation fails with a general error.
exceptions.message: Exception :\nBinding operation for user : {sBindUserName} failed with error : \n{message}

exceptions.trigger: The connection to the LDAP server fails.

exceptions.message: The variable ldapDistinguishedNameStartSearch is empty. The 'DN start search' value is mandatory!
Error : searching for: {sSearchUserName}; start search location: {sLdapDistinguishedNameStartSearch} filter: {sSearchFilter}\nreturned an error: \n{message}

exceptions.trigger: The search returns zero or more than one entry.
exceptions.message: The search returned : {count} entries for the search filter : {resolvedFilter}

exceptions.trigger: The matched entry's DN cannot be read.
exceptions.message: Cannot extract the DN from the search response for the search filter : {sSearchFilter}

caveats: Custom filters are formatted with sSearchUserName before execution. If your filter does not contain a {0} placeholder, the supplied sSearchUserName is ignored by the filter string itself.
The function always performs a subtree search from the starting DN.

do: Pass a dedicated bind account with a real password and enough rights to search the target branch.
Start from the narrowest practical base DN so the search is more likely to return exactly one entry.
Use a custom sSearchFilter only when you need a different attribute match, and keep it compatible with string.Format substitution.
dont: Treat sBindUserPassword as safely optional in practice. Omitting it passes an empty string and the bind fails.
Use an overly broad filter or base DN when you need one user. The function raises an error unless the search returns exactly one entry.
Assume sSearchUserName is validated for you. An empty value can still run through the search path and usually ends in a zero-results error.

elements.title: Second
elements.summary: Extracts the seconds component from a date value.

exceptions.trigger: dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: Argument: dDate must be of date type

caveats: Times that fall exactly on hh:mm:00 also return 0, so the result alone does not distinguish those values from an empty date.

do: Pass a real date value before calling Second.
Treat a returned 0 carefully when empty dates are possible, because a valid timestamp at hh:mm:00 also returns 0.
Combine Second with Hour or Minute when second-only precision is not enough.
dont: Pass NIL and expect 0; NIL raises an error.
Pass strings or other non-date values and expect implicit conversion.
Assume 0 always means an exact minute boundary when your input may be empty.

elements.title: Seconds
elements.summary: Returns the current time of day as the number of whole seconds since midnight.
caveats: The value is based only on the current day, so it wraps back to 0 at midnight.
Repeated calls can return different values as time advances.

do: Use Seconds() for same-day elapsed-time checks and daily schedule comparisons.
Capture the value once and reuse it when multiple checks should use the same timestamp.
Add explicit midnight-rollover handling when comparing a later Seconds() value to an earlier one.
dont: Use Seconds() by itself for elapsed time that can span more than one day.
Treat the result as a date or full timestamp. It only represents the current time of day.
Call Seconds() repeatedly inside one logical decision if a single consistent value is required.

elements.title: SendFromOutbox
elements.summary: Sends every email currently queued in the outbox.
caveats: Failed rows are recorded and processing continues with the next queued email.

do: Check the return value, because one batch can contain both successful and failed sends.
Review the remaining outbox entries after a .F. result and retry them after fixing the cause.
Use this function for queue-draining jobs after messages have already been added with SendToOutbox.
dont: Assume a .F. return means nothing was sent. Earlier rows may already have been delivered and removed from the outbox.
Assume the function stops at the first failure. It continues through the full outbox.
Use this function to compose or queue new messages. It only processes messages that are already in the outbox.

elements.title: SendLimsEmail
elements.summary: Sends an email through SMTP and returns whether the send succeeded.

exceptions.trigger: The SMTP server name is blank or otherwise invalid.
An attachment path in aAttachList is empty.
An attachment file does not exist.
exceptions.message: The following file was not found : <path>

exceptions.trigger: sEncryptedData cannot be deserialized into certificate information.
exceptions.message: The Certificate Info cannot be deserialized.

caveats: The first three parameters are required by contract, and blank values can also fail even when the parameter itself is not NIL.
bIgnoreErrors defaults to .T., so callers that need fail-fast behavior should pass .F. explicitly.

do: Pass a non-empty SMTP server name, recipient array, sender address, and subject.
Keep recipient, CC, BCC, and attachment values in arrays, even when only one item is needed.
Set bUseSSL to .T. when the SMTP server requires a secure connection.
Check the boolean return value when bIgnoreErrors is .T..
Validate attachment paths before calling the function.
dont: Rely on the surfaced optional signature to mean blank required fields are safe. Empty SMTP, sender, recipient, and subject values can still fail at runtime.
Pass a string where the function expects an array of addresses or attachments.
Ignore a .F. return when bIgnoreErrors is .T.. That means the send failed and only the error handling mode changed.
Mark HTML content as plain text. Set bIsBodyHTML correctly so the message body is interpreted as intended.
Pass attachment paths that are blank or missing on disk.

elements.title: SendOutlookReminder
elements.summary: Sends an Outlook-style meeting invitation email and returns whether delivery succeeded.
caveats: The function does not validate that comma-separated attendee name and email lists have the same length before building recipients.
Empty attendee email entries are skipped while the function builds the recipient list.
The generated invitation includes a built-in 15-minute display reminder.

do: Pass non-empty sSMTP, sSubject, sSummary, organizer, and attendee values.
Keep comma-separated attendee names and email addresses in the same order and count.
Pass "" for sLocation when the invite has no location instead of omitting the parameter.
Set bIgnoreErrors to .F. when the caller must stop on delivery or configuration failures.
Supply nPort, sUName, sUPass, and bUseSSL explicitly when your SMTP server requires them.
dont: Omit bIgnoreErrors. It is required and has no default.
Pass blank organizer or attendee fields. The function rejects them.
Let comma-separated attendee names and email addresses drift out of sync. That can produce failed or mispaired recipients.
Assume a .F. return means the function validated everything up front. It only means the send failed and the error was suppressed.

elements.title: SendToFtp
elements.summary: Uploads one local file to an FTP or SFTP server.

exceptions.trigger: sServerNameOrIP is NIL or empty in FTP mode.
exceptions.message: FTP server name or IP cannot be missing.

exceptions.trigger: sServerNameOrIP is NIL or empty in SFTP mode.
exceptions.message: SFTP server name or IP cannot be missing.
Local file name cannot be missing.
The proxy parameter cannot be used. The system will automatically use the proxy specified by Internet Explorer > Local Area Network settings.

caveats: The function does not validate sRemoteDirectory before delegating to the FTP or SFTP backend.

do: Validate sServerNameOrIP and sLocalFileName before calling so argument errors are caught earlier in your workflow.
Pass 22 explicitly for SFTP servers that use the standard SFTP port.
Check the boolean result and treat .F. as a failed upload that needs user feedback, retry logic, or follow-up handling.
Use sPrivateKeyFilePath in SFTP mode when the target server requires key-based authentication.
dont: Pass a non-empty sProxy value. The function rejects it in both FTP and SFTP mode.
Assume bUsePassive affects SFTP. It is only used by the FTP branch.
Assume the function always keeps the original local file name on the server. An explicit sRemoteFileName overrides it.
Rely on the default port for SFTP. The default fallback is 21, not 22.

elements.title: SendToOutbox
elements.summary: Queues an email request in LIMSEMAILOUTBOX for later delivery instead of sending it immediately.
caveats: The bIgnoreErrors parameter is accepted in the signature but does not suppress the required-argument exceptions. Treat those three throws as unconditional.

do: Validate sSMTP, aRecipients, and sFromWho before calling so you do not depend on runtime argument errors.
Pass explicit arrays for attachments, CC, and BCC when those lists are part of the workflow.
Use SendFromOutbox as the follow-up step when queued messages should actually be delivered.
Set bIgnoreErrors to .F. when the caller should surface and handle validation failures explicitly.
dont: Treat a successful return as proof that the email was sent. It only means the request was queued.
Pass "" for sSMTP or sFromWho, or pass an empty recipient array. Those values are still invalid.
Use SendToOutbox when the requirement is immediate delivery. Use SendLimsEmail for direct send behavior.
Assume bIgnoreErrors suppresses every failure. Its special handling only covers NIL required arguments before the queue call proceeds.

elements.title: ServerEndOfDay
elements.summary: Returns a date value set to the end of its day.

exceptions.trigger: dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: Argument: dDate must be of date type

caveats: The 23:59:59.997 time includes milliseconds, so it stops just short of the next calendar day — appropriate for inclusive range comparisons at millisecond precision.

do: Use this function to create an inclusive end-of-day boundary for server-side logic.
Pair it with ServerStartOfDay when you need a full-day range.
Handle empty dates deliberately if your workflow distinguishes between no date and a real cutoff.
dont: Pass strings, numbers, or other non-date values. The function raises an error instead of converting them.
Treat an empty date as if it were a computed end-of-day value. Empty dates are returned unchanged.
Use this when the cutoff must follow the user's local day. Call ClientEndOfDay instead.

elements.title: ServerStartOfDay
elements.summary: Returns a date value set to the start of its day.

exceptions.trigger: dDate is not a date value.
exceptions.message: Argument: dDate must be of date type

caveats: Unlike ClientStartOfDay, this function does not apply client timezone offset logic.

do: Use this function to create a server-side day start before filtering or grouping records.
Pair it with ServerEndOfDay when you need an inclusive full-day range.
Handle empty dates deliberately if your workflow distinguishes between no date and a real boundary.
dont: Pass strings, numbers, or other non-date values. The function raises an error instead of converting them.
Treat an empty date as if it were a computed midnight value. Empty dates are returned unchanged.
Use this when the boundary must follow the user's local day. Call ClientStartOfDay instead.

elements.title: ServerTimeZone
elements.summary: Returns the current server's UTC offset in minutes as a number.
caveats: The value is based on the server's current local time, so it can change when the server timezone or daylight saving offset changes.
The result is a minute offset, not a timezone name.
The offset can include non-hour increments such as 30-minute differences.

do: Use ServerTimeZone() when logic must follow the server's local timezone rather than the current user's timezone.
Capture the value once per logical operation if multiple calculations must use the same offset.
Pair it with UserTimeZone() when you need to explain or handle server-versus-user time differences.
dont: Assume the server offset matches the current user's local timezone.
Treat the offset as a full timestamp or a complete timezone identity.
Assume the value stays constant across long periods or daylight saving transitions.

elements.title: SetByName
elements.summary: Assigns a value to a variable whose name is supplied at runtime.

exceptions.trigger: No matching variable exists and undeclared-variable creation is disabled.

caveats: Variable names are resolved case-insensitively.
If the name exists in a caller scope but not the current scope, SetByName() updates the caller variable.
If the name does not match any local or public variable and undeclared-variable creation is enabled, a new local variable is created in the current scope.
If the name does not match any local or public variable and undeclared-variable creation is disabled, the call raises an error.
When a variable is resolved in a caller scope or created after a miss, the runtime's undefined-variable callback may run if one has been registered.

do: Validate or normalize the variable name before calling SetByName().
Use GetByName when you need to confirm the final value after a dynamic write.
Prefer CreateLocal or CreatePublic when you explicitly want to create a variable in a known scope.
Be deliberate about scope, because SetByName() can update a caller variable instead of creating a new current-scope local.
dont: Pass an empty name or unchecked input directly into SetByName().
Assume the function always creates a new variable. It only does that when undeclared-variable creation is enabled.
Use SetByName() when a direct variable assignment is already possible and clearer.
Assume the target is always in the current local scope. The runtime may update a caller or public variable instead.

elements.title: SetDecimalSeparator
elements.summary: Sets the current decimal separator and returns the previous setting.

exceptions.trigger: sDecimalSep is empty or has more than one character.

caveats: This function changes a shared decimal separator setting.
Unlike GetDecimalSep, this function does not provide a . fallback for an empty current setting before the change.

do: Save the returned value when you need to restore the previous separator later.
Pass a one-character string such as "." or ",".
Use GetDecimalSeparator when you need to check the current setting without changing it.
dont: Pass NIL, "", or a multi-character string such as "..".
Change the separator temporarily without restoring the previous value afterward.
Use GetDecimalSep when you need the separator as a string. That function returns a numeric character code, not the string value.

elements.title: SetDefaultConnection
elements.summary: Changes the active default database connection name and returns the previous default connection.

exceptions.message: Argument: sDefaultConnection must be a non-null string

caveats: An empty string is accepted because it is still a string.
Later database operations that rely on the default connection may fail if the stored value is not usable.

do: Save the current default with GetDefaultConnection before switching to another connection.
Restore the previous default in cleanup code when the connection change is temporary.
Treat the argument as a connection name only. Verify availability separately if your script depends on that connection being usable.
dont: Pass NIL, numbers, arrays, or objects. The function only accepts non-null strings.
Assume this function confirms that the target connection exists or is currently connected. It only stores the supplied string.
Change the default connection in shared workflow code without restoring the previous value when later steps depend on the original default.

elements.title: SetGroupSeparator
elements.summary: Changes the group (thousands) separator character used when numbers are formatted as strings across the application.

exceptions.trigger: sGroupSep is empty or not exactly one character.

caveats: Changing the separator affects all subsequent numeric operations globally; parts of the application that expect a different separator may behave incorrectly.
There is no validation that sGroupSep does not conflict with the current decimal separator.

do: Always provide a single, non-empty character as the separator.
Check the need for separator change at application startup or user profile update.
Use GetGroupSeparator to retrieve the current separator before changing it, especially in shared environments.
dont: Pass multi-character strings or empty values as sGroupSep.
Frequently switch the separator mid-session unless all components are aware of the change; the setting is global and unpredictable changes can cause inconsistent parsing.
Assume the initial separator value without querying; other modules may have changed it.

elements.title: SetInternal
elements.summary: Assigns a value to a named property on a target value and returns NIL.
caveats: Any additional behavior depends on the target value's own property-set logic.

do: Check that the target value and property name are available before calling.
Use SetInternal when the property name is stored in a variable or built dynamically.
Read the property back with GetInternal when later logic depends on the assigned value.
dont: Pass NIL for oTarget or sPropName. The function raises an error instead of doing nothing.
Expect a confirmation value from SetInternal. It always returns NIL.
Use SetInternal for indexed collection updates. Use SetInternalC when you need indexed assignment.

elements.title: SetInternalC
elements.summary: Assigns a value through a chained index path on a target value and returns NIL.
caveats: SetInternalC writes at the last supplied non-null index argument.
Any failure beyond the null checks on oTarget, sCollectionName, and vArg1

do: Pass only the index depth you actually need, and omit unused trailing arguments.
Read the current value with GetInternalC first when overwriting a nested location matters.
Validate that each earlier step in the path is valid for the target value before assigning deeper into it.
dont: Pass NIL for oTarget, sCollectionName, or vArg1. Those inputs raise an immediate error.
Supply deeper index arguments unless the target really supports that full path.
Expect SetInternalC to return the assigned value or a success flag. It always returns NIL.

elements.title: SetSqlTimeout
elements.summary: Sets the SQL command timeout for a database connection and returns the previous timeout value for that same connection.

exceptions.trigger: nTimeout is supplied but is not an integer-valued number.
sConnection is supplied but is not a string.

caveats: This function changes the timeout on the targeted connection immediately.

do: Save the returned timeout value and restore it after the temporary operation finishes.
Keep timeout changes scoped to the smallest practical block of database work.
Pass an explicit connection name when the script works with more than one database connection.
dont: Pass fractional values such as 3.5; the function rejects non-integer numbers.
Assume a timeout change resets automatically after the next query.
Change one named connection and expect the timeout on other connections to change with it.

elements.title: SetUserData
elements.summary: Sets the current user name for the active execution context.

exceptions.trigger: sUserName is NIL, not a string, or empty.
exceptions.message: Argument: sUserName must be a non-empty string.

caveats: The change affects the current execution context immediately.

do: Save the current user with GetUserData before changing it when you need to restore it later.
Pass a known non-empty string value.
Keep the scope of the user change as small as practical.
dont: Pass NIL, an empty string, or a non-string value.
Assume this function authenticates the user or verifies that the user exists.
Change the current user without restoring the previous value when later code depends on the original context.

elements.title: SetUserPassword
elements.summary: Updates a user's password and returns the stored password hash.
caveats: Both sUserName and sPassword throw the same exception message when NIL is passed.

do: Validate both arguments before calling, especially when empty strings are not acceptable in your workflow.
Use ChkPassword to re-authenticate a user before changing an existing password.
Use ChkNewPassword separately when you need password-history checks.
Check the return value and treat "" as an update failure.
dont: Assume this function enforces password complexity, password history, or other policy rules.
Assume empty strings raise an exception. Only NIL is rejected by the documented implementation.
Ignore the return value when the password change must be confirmed.
Expose the new password carelessly in current-user flows, because the session is updated when sUserName matches the active user.

elements.title: ShowSqlErrors
elements.summary: Sets the SQL error display flag and returns the previous setting.
caveats: The setting remains in effect for later SQL work until you change it again.
This setting can affect behavior together with IgnoreSqlErrors. Fatal SQL errors can still be raised when SQL error suppression is enabled if SQL error display is also enabled.

do: Save the returned value and restore it after the temporary database work is complete.
Keep the setting change scoped to a small, clearly defined block of work.
Use :TRY and :FINALLY when later code could fail before the original setting is restored.
dont: Leave the flag changed longer than necessary when the intent was only temporary troubleshooting.
Assume the setting resets automatically after the next SQL statement.
Change this flag without considering IgnoreSqlErrors when you need predictable SQL error handling behavior.

elements.title: SigFig
elements.summary: Returns a string produced by applying a named rounding standard to a numeric value.
caveats: The result string replaces . with the system decimal separator before returning.
Negative values keep the minus sign in the returned string.
For ISO, the returned string can be left-padded to a total length of 15 characters.

do: Pass an explicit supported standard such as FDA, EPA, or ISO.
Treat the return value as a string, especially before concatenating, exporting, or storing it.
Compare the result with StdRound when reviewing legacy code, because both functions produce the same output.
Trim the result when needed before fixed-width export handling, especially for ISO output.
dont: Assume SigFig returns a numeric value. It returns formatted text, which can affect later calculations.
Pass NIL for any argument. The function raises an error instead of supplying defaults.
Rely on undocumented standard names. The verified implementation only branches on FDA, EPA, and ISO.
Use SigFig as a drop-in replacement for every ordinary rounding need when plain numeric rounding is sufficient.

elements.title: SortArray
elements.summary: Sorts an array in place and returns the same array.

exceptions.trigger: The custom comparator does not return a numeric comparison result.
exceptions.message: Array sort: the code block which compares two elements of the array, needs to return an integer value (less than zero if first element is smaller; zero if they are equal; and greater than zero if first element is greater).

caveats: Only .T. and numeric 1 select the numeric comparer. Other truthy values fall back to the default sort.

do: Use .T. or 1 when you want numeric comparison explicitly.
Use a two-argument code block when sorting arrays of rows or objects.
Remember that the function mutates aTarget and returns that same array.
dont: Assume the code block receives one element. It compares two elements and must return a negative number, 0, or a positive number.
Expect SortArray to leave the original array unchanged. Any other reference to the same array sees the new order.
Expect unsupported error behavior for other vNumeric values. Values other than .T., 1, or a code block fall back to the default sort path.

elements.title: Sqrt
elements.summary: Calculates the square root of a number.

do: Check whether the input can be negative when downstream logic cannot tolerate NaN.
Use Sqrt directly when the operation is known in advance.
Validate or normalize incoming values before calling when the source data may be incomplete.
dont: Assume a negative input returns 0 or raises a domain exception. It returns NaN.
Pass a value that might be NIL without checking first. The call
Use MatFunc just to wrap a fixed square-root call.

elements.title: StdRound
elements.summary: Returns a string produced by applying a named rounding standard to a numeric value.
caveats: For ISO, the returned string is truncated to 15 characters when it becomes longer than that.
nDigits is parsed as an integer by the implementation, so fractional values are not a supported input shape.
Unsupported sStandard values are not rejected. They skip the named rounding branches and still return a formatted string.

do: Pass one of the supported standard names: FDA, EPA, or ISO.
Pass nDigits as a whole-number value.
Treat the result as a string, especially before concatenating, exporting, or storing it.
Trim ISO output when you need a display value without the fixed-width left padding.
Use Round instead when you need an ordinary numeric rounding result.
dont: Assume StdRound returns a number. It returns formatted text.
Pass NIL for any argument. The function raises an error instead
Rely on undocumented standard names or alternate casing. The function only branches on exact FDA, EPA, and ISO values.
Assume nDigits means the same thing for every standard. The EPA path does not behave like a simple decimal-place formatter.

elements.title: Str
elements.summary: Converts a numeric value to a formatted string.
caveats: If the formatted value does not fit in the requested width, Str() returns a string of * characters of that width.
When nLength is supplied and nDecimals is omitted, the function keeps fractional digits only as long as they fit in the field.
Negative values are padded so that the minus sign stays immediately before the first visible digit.
A negative nLength uses the current numeric separator settings when formatting the result. See SetDecimalSeparator and SetGroupSeparator.

do: Specify both nLength and nDecimals when the output must have a predictable width and precision.
Trim the result with LTrim() if you want the formatted value without the leading padding.
Use LStr() when you need a simple trimmed conversion or need to handle NIL input without an error.
dont: Pass NIL as nNumber. Str() raises an error instead of returning a placeholder string.
Assume Str(nNumber) returns an unpadded value. Default output commonly includes leading spaces.
Ignore an all-asterisk result. That indicates the requested field width cannot represent the value.

elements.title: StrSrch
elements.summary: Finds a substring by occurrence number or from a specific 1-based starting position.

exceptions.trigger: indexOrOccurence is out of range for the selected mode.
exceptions.message: A negative [indexOrOccurence] value is not allowed.

caveats: In start-position mode, passing 0 raises the same out-of-range error used for negative values.

do: Use the default call form when you want the first occurrence.
Pass 2, 3, or higher with flag set to .F. when you need a later occurrence.
Pass a known position with flag set to .T. when continuing a staged parse.
Check for 0 before using the result as a string position.
dont: Assume the result is zero-based. A match at the first character returns 1.
Assume indexOrOccurence always means the same thing. Its meaning changes when flag changes.
Pass NIL for subStr or source. Null inputs raise a runtime error.
Use StrSrch() when At already covers your case. StrSrch() is most useful when you need a later occurrence or a controlled starting position.

elements.title: StrTran
elements.summary: Replaces all occurrences of a specified substring with another substring in a source string.
caveats: Replacements are non-overlapping. Each match is processed once in the original left-to-right scan.
The raw source does not define a special searchFor = "" behavior. Avoid empty search strings.

do: Validate that source and searchFor are not NIL before calling.
Use StrTran() when you need exact case-sensitive matching.
Pass "" when you want to remove all occurrences of the search substring.
Use Replace instead when you want case-insensitive matching.
dont: Pass NIL for source or searchFor; StrTran() raises an error.
Use StrTran() when you expect case-insensitive matching.
Use it for pattern or wildcard replacement; it only matches literal substrings.

elements.title: StrZero
elements.summary: Formats a number as a zero-padded string, with optional total width and decimal precision.

exceptions.trigger: nNumber is NIL and at least one optional argument is supplied.

caveats: If all three arguments are NIL, StrZero returns an empty string without raising.
If both nLength and nDecimals are negative, StrZero falls back to the default width and decimal behavior.
When nDecimals is supplied, the output uses the current SSL decimal separator setting.

do: Specify nLength and nDecimals explicitly when an export or interface requires a fixed format.
Check for an all-asterisk result when the value might not fit the requested width.
Validate that nNumber is not NIL before calling StrZero.
dont: Rely on the default width when downstream code expects a specific field size.
Treat an all-asterisk result as valid formatted data.
Pass NIL for nNumber; depending on the other arguments, the function either raises or returns an empty string.

elements.title: StringToDate
elements.summary: Converts a formatted date string into a date value using a specified pattern.
caveats: Parsing uses invariant culture, so month-name formats such as dd-MMM-yyyy depend on invariant-culture month text.
The format is normalized before parsing, so common patterns such as MM/dd/yyyy and dd-MM-yyyy accept one- or two-digit month/day input.

do: Check Empty(dParsedDate) after calling StringToDate() so failed parses do not silently flow into later logic.
Keep the expected format in one place, such as a local variable or configuration value, when multiple rows use the same pattern.
Prefer unambiguous formats such as yyyy-MM-dd or yyyyMMdd when you control the input format.
dont: Assume a failed parse will throw. Format mismatches return an empty date, so :CATCH will not handle ordinary bad input.
Pass NIL for either argument. Those cases raise immediately instead of returning an empty date.
Rely on locale-dependent expectations when a stable external format is available. StringToDate() uses invariant-culture parsing.

elements.title: SubStr
elements.summary: Extracts part of a string starting at a position you specify.
caveats: A negative nStartPos that would go before the start of the string clamps to position 1.

do: Check that the input string is available before calling SubStr().
Use negative nStartPos values when you want characters relative to the end of the string.
Omit nLength when you want everything from the start position to the end.
dont: Pass NIL as sSource. That raises an error instead of returning an empty string.
Over-calculate end offsets when a negative nStartPos already expresses the intent clearly.
Add manual bounds logic just to reach the end of the string when omitting nLength already does that.

elements.title: SubmitToBatch
elements.summary: Submits SSL code to a batch worker and returns the submitted job identifier.
caveats: The submitted batch receives the supplied script parameters plus the current session and sticky variables.
An object passed through vParameters is not unpacked generically. Only Parameters and Caption affect submission.
SubmitToBatch() does not wait for the batch job to complete before returning.

elements.title: TableFldLst

elements.title: UsrMes
