Read ParentTable — the property is write-only.

elements.title: CDataField
elements.summary: Represents one field in a CDataRow.
caveats: For non-binary fields, assigning NIL stores a null value.
ToNumber() may raise an error when the current value is not numeric and is not a parsable string.
For binary fields, reading Value depends on a usable table import folder.
For binary fields, assigning Value requires a string file path that already exists.

do: Get CDataField instances by calling GetField() on a CDataRow so the field stays tied to a real row and column.
Check IsNull before formatting or converting values that may be null.
Use ToDate(), ToNumber(), or ToString() when you want explicit type handling instead of relying on Value.
Set Value := NIL when you want to clear a non-binary field.
Make sure the parent table has a valid ImportFolder before reading a binary field through Value.
dont: Treat GetSqlFormattedValue() as a safe replacement for parameterized SQL — it only formats the current value and does not bind parameters or escape every SQL case.
Assume ToString() converts every value to text — it returns NIL for non-string values.
Assume ToDate() accepts arbitrary date formats — it only parses MM/dd/yyyy and yyyy-MM-dd strings.
Assign a non-string value to a binary field — binary assignment expects a file path and raises an error for the wrong type.
Assign a missing file path to a binary field — the assignment fails when the file does not exist.
Expect ToNumber() to convert arbitrary value types — non-string, non-numeric values can fail instead of returning NIL.

elements.title: CDataRow
elements.summary: Represents one row in a CDataTable.
caveats: Accessing a property by column name (e.g. oRow:status) returns a CDataField object, not the raw field value. Use :Value to read or assign the actual data.

do: Use GetField() when you need a specific column by name or position.
Use GetField(...):Value to read or assign the actual field value.
Use GetPKsValues() when you need the row's primary-key values.
Pair ToArrayForInsert() with CDataTable:GetInsertSql().
Pair ToArrayForUpdate() with CDataTable:GetUpdateSql().
Convert GetIndex() results before passing them to GetField().
dont: Treat GetIndex() as a 1-based SSL array index — it returns a zero-based position and -1 when the column is missing.
Treat row property access as the raw field value — it resolves to a CDataField; use :Value for the actual data.
Use ToArray() for insert or update parameter binding — it includes all columns and does not match the filtered ordering used by the table SQL helpers.
Assume ToArrayForUpdate() excludes primary keys — it appends primary-key values after the non-primary-key values to match the generated WHERE clause.
Expect binary columns in ToArrayForInsert() or ToArrayForUpdate() — both helpers omit them.

elements.title: CDataTable
elements.summary: Provides an in-memory table object for working with rows, columns, XML, and database persistence from SSL.
caveats: Setting IsSystem, Name, or NullAsBlank to NIL raises an error.
Setting ImportFolder to NIL clears it to an empty string.
Select({}, {}) returns an empty result set, not all rows.
GetInsertSql() and GetUpdateSql() skip binary columns.
SaveToDb() can still include binary columns unless you omit them with aSkipColumns.
UpdateFieldFromArray() returns .T. even when no row values match the provided keys.

do: Use GetTable() from TablesImport or FromXml() when you need a CDataTable with a working schema already in place.
Call AddPK() before using GetUpdateSql() or relying on update behavior in SaveToDb().
Use Select({fieldNames}, {valueArrays}) when you want predictable exact field/value matching from SSL code.
Use Rows when you want every row in the table, and ToArray() when you want plain value arrays instead of CDataRow objects.
Set NullAsBlank deliberately before exporting to arrays or XML so null handling matches your script's expectations.
dont: Pass NIL as the first argument to Select() to mean "all rows" — that raises An array of names is expected!.
Assume ToArray() returns CDataRow objects — it returns a plain 2D array of field values.
Expect SaveToDb() to audit changes just because you passed bDoAudit — that parameter does not change the method's behavior.
Rely on ErrMsg for method failures on this class — the documented methods raise errors directly and do not populate that property.
Expect CDataTable{sName, nColCount} to build the schema — the constructor only sets the table name.

elements.title: Email
elements.summary: Composes, loads, saves, sends, or queues email messages with attachments and optional signing or encryption.
caveats: LoadMessage and SaveMessage require a non-empty file path.
If SMTPServerUserName is empty, Send connects without attempting SMTP login.
After a successful LoadMessage, Send transmits the loaded message content with the current SMTP settings; SaveMessage and SendToOutbox always use the current property values, not the loaded content.

do: Set From, To, Subject, and Body before calling Send, SaveMessage, or SendToOutbox.
Set To to a non-empty array of recipient addresses.
Set SMTPServerName and SMTPServerPort before calling Send or SendToOutbox.
Use Email{} when you want non-throwing behavior and plan to inspect oEmail:Exception:Description after a failure.
Use Email{.F.} inside :TRY and handle failures with GetLastSSLError() when the script should stop on email errors.
Validate attachment paths and certificate inputs before sending or queueing mail.
Use SendToOutbox when delivery should be deferred to later processing.
dont: Assume Email{} raises on failure — it suppresses exceptions by default and returns .F. instead.
Leave To unset or empty before Send, SaveMessage, or SendToOutbox.
Omit required message fields — the message must include From, Subject, and Body before it can be built.
Omit SMTPServerName or set SMTPServerPort to 0 when calling Send or SendToOutbox — SMTP validation fails.
Rely on SMTPTimeout or LogSMTP for queued outbox delivery — those settings apply to direct Send behavior, not later outbox processing.
Pass an invalid certificate store name, an unknown certificate email address, or a missing certificate file — the certificate methods fail and the email cannot be signed or encrypted.
Expect LoadMessage to populate the editable properties used by SaveMessage or SendToOutbox — loaded message content is only reused by Send.

elements.title: EnterpriseExporter
elements.summary: Exports tables into a destination folder.
caveats: In default mode, an empty table list exports all regular tables or all system tables, depending on bSysTables.
In enterprise-only mode and SQL mode, an empty table list fails.
If both IsEnterpriseOnly and FromSQL are set to .T., enterprise-only mode takes precedence.
If a table export fails after its output folder is created, that table's output folder is removed.
An undefined table or a table with no defined fields can raise an error instead of only returning .F..
ErrorMsg reports the final exporter message, which can be blank even after an earlier table failed.

do: Pass a destination folder, not a file name.
Use a simple array of table names for default-mode exports.
Use table-entry arrays when IsEnterpriseOnly or FromSQL is enabled.
In enterprise-only mode, use the second entry value as the FROM source when you need a custom source per exported table.
In SQL mode, use the second entry value for the full SQL statement you want exported for that table.
Set LogFile when you need per-table confirmation and troubleshooting.
Use NullAsBlank and InvariantDateColumns only with FromSQL.
Use :TRY and GetLastSSLError if your script must handle invalid table definitions safely.
dont: Describe sPath as a single export file. The exporter creates a subfolder for each table and writes that table's text file inside it.
Assume an empty aTables array always means "export everything." That only happens in default mode.
Use a one-dimensional table list with IsEnterpriseOnly or FromSQL. Those modes expect table entries.
Treat the second value in a table entry as arbitrary metadata. In enterprise-only mode it is the FROM source, and in SQL mode it is the full SQL statement.
Rely on AbortOnError to stop the run. Setting it does not affect DoExport().
Treat DoExport() by itself as a per-table success report for multi-table runs. Use the log file when you need table-by-table confirmation.
Expect NullAsBlank or InvariantDateColumns to affect default or enterprise-only exports. They are used only in SQL mode.
Assume every bad table definition only returns .F.. Undefined tables can raise an error during export setup.

elements.title: FtpsClient
elements.summary: Transfers files and manages directories on an FTPS server.
caveats: CheckOnFtps checks for a file, not for directory existence.
CopyToFtps writes identical content to every name in aRemoteFileNames.
GetDirFromFtps returns rows in the shape {name, size, date, time, attributes}, with date and time returned as strings.
GetDirNamesFromFtps and GetDirFromFtps return NIL when the server request fails.
ReadFromFtps returns "" on failure.
SendToFtps uses the local file name when sRemoteFileName is empty.
GetFromFtps uses the remote file name when sLocalFileName is empty.

do: Configure proxy and TLS settings before Connect so the session starts with the intended network and certificate behavior.
Call Connect, then Login, then Secure only when that matches the server workflow you need.
Check boolean return values after file and directory operations so your script can stop or recover cleanly.
Validate remote file names, directory paths, and local file paths before
Call Disconnect when the transfer session is complete.
Treat WriteToFtps as an append operation when the remote file already exists.
dont: Call file or directory methods before a successful connection and login. Those operations depend on an active authenticated session.
Pass empty file names, directory paths, or local file paths to methods that validate them. They can raise argument errors.
Assume WriteToFtps overwrites an existing file. The current behavior appends at the existing remote file length.
Rely on ReadFromFtps(maxSize) to enforce a hard read limit. The full file contents are read regardless of the nMaxSize argument.
Forget to disconnect. This class does not automatically close the session for you.

elements.title: HtmlConverter
elements.summary: Converts XFD form XML into HTML form XML and exposes the most recent conversion log.
caveats: Setting OptionsXml replaces the current conversion options. Passing an empty or invalid options XML string fails when the property is assigned.
Convert replaces the supplied sXfdGuid text in the source XML before it parses the form. Pass the form ID that is actually present in the XFD XML.
SimplifiedLog is a summary view, not a verbatim copy of Log. Use Log when you need the full message text from the last conversion.
ClearLog() clears only the stored log. It does not reset OptionsXml.

do: Set OptionsXml before Convert when the conversion requires non-default options.
Read Log or SimplifiedLog after Convert when you need conversion details.
Call ClearLog() before a separate workflow if you want the next log to start empty.
dont: Call Convert again before reading the current log if you still need it — each conversion replaces the stored log.
Assume conversion problems are always reported only in the log — invalid option XML or invalid input XML can fail the operation itself.
Set OptionsXml after Convert and expect it to affect the conversion that already ran — options apply to later conversions.

elements.title: PatcherSupport
elements.summary: Provides helper methods for collecting package-style dictionary metadata, connecting to another STARLIMS system, and comparing one collected result table to another.
caveats: Compare() updates both ResultTable and DiffDataTable when it succeeds.
GetAllFormsFromDictionary_Test() is a narrow helper that loads only form metadata.
InternalErrors is not the same as the trace log. It captures accumulated error text, while LogFilePath controls file-based trace output.

do: Populate ResultTable before calling Compare() so the comparison has a source table to work with.
Check the boolean result from ConnectToExternalSystem() before trying to read remote dictionary content.
Read InternalErrors after failed metadata retrieval or log-path setup so you can diagnose what went wrong.
Set LogFilePath before long collection or comparison runs when you want a fresh trace file for that run.
Use DiffDataTable after Compare() when you need a serialized copy of the remaining differences.
dont: Call Compare() with a non-table value or with missing tables — the method returns NIL instead of producing comparison output.
Assume Compare() returns every row from the source table — unchanged rows are removed from the current result set.
Treat ConnectToExternalSystem() failures as thrown errors — the method reports failure with .F..
Expect GetDataFromWholeDictionary() to clear earlier rows first — it appends collected metadata to the existing ResultTable.
Assume setting LogFilePath always succeeds — invalid or inaccessible paths are recorded in InternalErrors and the trace listeners are only updated on success.

elements.title: PdfSupport
elements.summary: Provides methods to create, modify, secure, save, and print PDF documents.
caveats: Use page numbers from 1 through PageCount. Page numbers outside that range are not supported.

DocumentSecurityLevel only accepts None, Encrypted40Bit, or Encrypted128Bit; other values throw DocumentSecurityLevel must be a string and have one of the values: None, Encrypted40Bit or Encrypted128Bit.
SetTextStyle expects a recognized font style name and a valid color name.
Print prints the file path you pass to it, not the in-memory document.

do: Set document passwords and permission properties before Save so the saved PDF includes those settings.
Use a positive integer page number that already exists before calling AddTextOnPage.
Call SetTextStyle before AddTextOnPage when you need a font, size, style, or color other than the default Verdana 20 black text.
Use Protect when its built-in security profile matches your needs, then adjust permission properties before Save if you need different settings.
Pass a valid Adobe Reader executable path, a valid PDF file path, and a valid printer name to Print.
dont: Change passwords or permission properties after Save and expect an already-written file to change — those settings only take effect when the document is saved again.
Call AddPageFromImage with a missing image file — it throws File not found!.
Pass a page number that is non-integer or beyond the current page count to AddTextOnPage — it throws Page number is invalid!.
Rely on page number 0 or negative page numbers — the method expects an existing page and those values are outside the supported usage.
Expect SetTextStyle to change text that was already drawn — it only affects later AddTextOnPage calls.
Assume Protect preserves previously assigned passwords or permission values — it overwrites them with its own preset security settings.
Treat Print as saving or printing the in-memory document automatically — it prints the file path you pass to the method.

elements.title: RegSetup
elements.summary: Provides access to Windows registry values under HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE.
caveats: RegCloseKey() always returns .T., so its return value does not tell you whether a key was previously open.

do: Check the return value of RegOpenKey() before calling RegQueryValue().
Pass registry paths relative to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE.
Treat RegQueryValue() results as dynamic values and validate them before use.
Call RegCloseKey() after finishing registry reads.
dont: Assume the nAccess argument changes the open mode — the current behavior does not use it.
Assume the nType argument converts the result — the returned value comes from the stored registry value.
Call RegQueryValue() before a successful RegOpenKey() — it raises an error.
Leave the key open after reading values — open handles should be released promptly.
Assume a missing value raises an error — RegQueryValue() returns NIL when the value name is not present.

elements.title: SDMS
elements.summary: Interacts with an external SDMS server to download documents, download Unified XML templates, create an SDMSDocUploader, and generate password hashes for SDMS authentication.

exceptions.message: Method is deprecated, please do not use anymore.
Method is deprecated, please do not use anymore.
Method is deprecated, please do not use anymore.
Method is deprecated, please do not use anymore.
Method is deprecated, please do not use anymore.

exceptions.trigger: DownloadUnifiedXmlTemplate is called with sTemplateId as NIL.
DownloadUnifiedXmlTemplate is called with sDestinationPath as NIL.

caveats: GetHttpPassHash and GetSoapPassHash expect a hexadecimal password string.
Failed download requests populate ErrorMessage with a message starting with Unable to download document file from SDMS:. A successful request clears ErrorMessage.
DownloadUnifiedXmlTemplate treats a numeric sTemplateId as a template ID; any other string is sent as a template name.
IsSessionExpired is reset to .F. before a successful response is returned. When a failed download reports Please relogin. You session may be expired., IsSessionExpired is set to .T..

do: Construct SDMS with a credentials object when you plan to download files.
Use DownloadOriginalDocument2, DownloadUnifiedXmlDocument2, or CheckOutDocument when one of those fixed document types matches your intent.
Check the boolean return value and ErrorMessage after each download, check-out, or template download request.
Check IsSessionExpired after a failed request so you can distinguish session expiry from other download failures.
Pass a numeric string for sTemplateId when you want to address a template by ID, or pass the template name when you want name-based lookup.
dont: Use deprecated methods such as DownloadDocument, DownloadOriginalDocument, DownloadUnifiedXmlDocument, SetSDMSConnection, or UploadDocument, because they always raise their deprecation exception.
Rely on SDMS{} alone for download operations, because the class only loads its URL, user name, password hash, site ID, and initial session ID when you construct it with a credentials object.
Assume a failed request means the session expired, because IsSessionExpired is the flag that distinguishes session-expiry failures from other download errors.
Pass NIL for sTemplateId or sDestinationPath to DownloadUnifiedXmlTemplate, because the method raises explicit argument exceptions for those values.
Ignore the exact meaning of sDestinationPath, because the download methods write to the path you provide rather than choosing a file name for you.

elements.title: SDMSDocUploader
elements.summary: Uploads files into SDMS, attaches uploads to workflow steps, and checks in document revisions.
caveats: UploadOriginalDoc(), AttachDocToWorkflow(), AttachFileToDocument(), UploadOfficeTemplate(), UploadELNDocument(), and UploadNewRevisionForWorkflowDocument() can auto-fill blank DocName, FileType, and ProjectName values.
CheckInDocument() does not auto-fill DocName; it raises an error if DocName is blank.
AttachDocToWorkflow() validates missing workflow IDs only when those properties are still in the missing-value state used by the credentialed constructor.
CheckInWorkflowDocument() accepts NIL for nDocEntryPoint; in that case it skips the source stage header.
DoUpload() returns the document ID, while the higher-level helper methods return booleans.
When UploadOriginalDoc() runs the UXML upload, FilePath is overwritten with a temporary path. Reassign FilePath before reusing the same uploader instance.
Large files are uploaded automatically in multiple chunks.

do: Use SDMSDocUploader{oCredentials} when the upload requires established SDMS connection and authentication values.
Set FilePath before calling any upload, attach, or check-in method that depends on the instance properties.
Set DocId and DocName before CheckInDocument() and set DocId before AttachFileToDocument().
Set WorkflowId, StageId, and ActionId before calling workflow-specific methods.
Check the boolean result or returned document ID after every call and inspect ErrorMessage when the operation fails.
Set both Metadata and UXmlTemplate before UploadOriginalDoc() when you need the related UXML upload.
dont: Assume the parameterless constructor supplies SDMS connection details. It does not load them for you.
Rely on workflow methods to infer missing workflow IDs. Missing-ID validation is only triggered in the cases documented above, and parameterless instances can pass zero-valued workflow IDs instead.
Call CheckInDocument() without FilePath, DocId, and DocName. That raises validation errors before the upload starts.
Expect Metadata or UXmlTemplate to affect workflow attachment, revision upload, Office template upload, or ELN upload. They are only used by UploadOriginalDoc().
Treat DoUpload() as a full replacement for the higher-level helpers. It uploads the file to a supplied SDMS URL, but it does not prepare the standard document or workflow context for you.

elements.title: SQLConnection
elements.summary: Represents a configured database connection returned by GetConnectionByName.
caveats: Password and ConnectionString can expose sensitive credentials. Avoid writing them to logs, UI messages, audit tables, or error details.
DatabaseName is only extracted for SQL Server connections. Other platforms may report "Not Set".
Property names are available through standard SSL colon-property access, for example oConn:Platforma.

do: Use GetConnectionByName when you need a SQLConnection object.
Read DataSource, DatabaseName, or Platforma for diagnostics instead of parsing the connection string yourself.
Treat UseUTC as connection metadata when formatting or comparing timestamp values.
dont: Display, log, or return Password or ConnectionString in user-facing messages.
Assume DatabaseName is populated for every platform. Oracle connections return "Not Set".

elements.title: SSLBaseDictionary
elements.summary: Provides the shared dictionary surface used by SSL dictionary classes such as SSLStringDictionary{} and SSLIntDictionary{}.
caveats: Invoke("Clear") works without arguments, but methods such as AddValue, GetValue, Contains, Remove, and TryGetValue raise an error when called through Invoke without the required key argument.

do: Create a concrete dictionary class that matches your key type, then use the inherited SSLBaseDictionary members on that instance.
Use AddValue(vKey, vValue) when you want invalid keys to fail with a boolean result instead of an exception.
Use GetValue(vKey, vDefaultValue) when you want a simple fallback value for missing keys.
Use TryGetValue(vKey) when you need to know both whether a key exists and what value was stored.
Read Keys, Values, and Count after updates when you need the current dictionary state.
dont: Document or write examples as if SSLBaseDictionary{} can be instantiated directly, because this page describes an abstract base class.
Assume all dictionary classes accept the same key types, because the concrete class decides what is valid.
Expect all invalid-key operations to fail the same way, because AddValue and GetValue handle invalid keys differently from Contains, Remove, and TryGetValue.
Treat TryGetValue as returning the value by itself, because it returns an object with Exists and Value.
Pass empty placeholder values just to fill optional arguments, because SSL lets you omit trailing optional arguments.

elements.title: SSLCodeProvider
elements.summary: Compiles published server scripts and data sources and returns the compilation results as an SSLCompilerErrorList.
caveats: Single-item compile methods require either a GUID or a full category.name.
Category methods take category IDs, not category names.
The class reports compile-time problems only. It does not validate runtime behavior.

do: Pass a full category.name or a GUID when targeting one server script or data source.
Use the bulk and category methods when validating larger changes.
Check the returned SSLCompilerErrorList even when you expect a clean compile.
Use category GUIDs with the ...Category and ...Categories methods.
dont: Pass a bare script or data source name without its category. Those calls are rejected unless you provide a GUID.
Use CompileScript for ad hoc code validation. It currently raises a not-implemented error for any non-null input.
Assume a successful call means every target compiled cleanly. Review the returned error list before continuing automation or deployment steps.

elements.title: SSLDataset
elements.summary: Represents dataset results so SSL code can work with query output as an object, convert the first table to an array, export XML, or pass the dataset handle to APIs that expect one.
caveats: The bNullAsBlank setting affects ToArray() output, not the underlying dataset itself.
ToXml() requires loaded dataset content. An empty SSLDataset{} does not provide a safe XML export.

do: Prefer GetSSLDataset or RunDS with "ssldataset" when you need a populated SSLDataset.
Pass bNullAsBlank explicitly when wrapping an existing dataset handle so the ToArray() behavior is clear.
Check ALen(oDataset:ToArray()) before assuming the first table contains rows.
Use ToDataSet() only when the next API specifically expects a dataset object instead of an SSL array or XML string.
dont: Assume ToArray() includes every table in a multi-table dataset. It only returns the first table.
Treat bNullAsBlank as optional in SSLDataset{xData, xNullAsBlank}. The wrapping constructor expects both arguments.
Call ToXml() on an empty SSLDataset{}. Load data first.
Use ToDataSet() unless the next API specifically needs a dataset handle. ToArray() or ToXml() are usually clearer in SSL code.

elements.title: SSLError
elements.summary: Represents an SSL error and exposes its message, location, code, formatted diagnostic text, and nested SSL error details.
caveats: InnerException only returns nested SSL runtime errors, not every possible nested cause.
Operation can be blank and Code can be 0 when the source error did not provide them.
FullDescription and FullDescriptionEx are formatted diagnostic strings, not structured field-by-field data.
FullDescriptionEx includes the full stack trace when one is available; FullDescription uses a shorter stack view.
For compiler errors, SQL errors, and script-not-found errors, the formatted diagnostic text uses specialized headings instead of a generic Error: line.
When constructing SSLError from an arbitrary exception, Operation defaults to blank and Code to 0 unless the wrapped exception already carried that information.

do: Read oErr:Description for user-facing error text after GetLastSSLError().
Use oErr:Code and oErr:Operation when you need structured diagnostics.
Use oErr:FullDescriptionEx for support logs when you need the complete stack trace.
dont: Assume oErr:InnerException is always available or that it contains every nested cause. Only nested SSL runtime errors surface there.
Show FullDescription or FullDescriptionEx directly to end users unless they need technical diagnostics.
Rely on Operation or Code being populated when you build an SSLError from an arbitrary exception object.

elements.title: SSLExpando
elements.summary: SSLExpando is a built-in object class for storing named values whose shape is decided at runtime.
caveats: GetPropList() returns only dynamic property names. It does not include inherited object members or XmlType.
IsProperty("XmlType") is always true, even if you never assigned a custom XML type.
When XmlType is not set, ToString() uses SSLExpando as the root element name. Setting XmlType to NIL clears any custom value and restores the default.
ToString() returns the same XML content as Serialize().
ToString() returns XML, not a debug-style property dump.

do: Use SetProperty() and GetProperty() when the property name comes from runtime data.
Set XmlType before serializing when another system expects a specific root element name.
Call IsProperty() before GetProperty() for optional fields.
dont: Assume GetProperty() returns an empty value for missing fields. It raises Property not found: <name>..
Expect GetPropList() to include inherited object members. It only returns dynamic properties on the SSLExpando.
Rely on clone() for an independent deep copy of nested arrays or objects. Only the top-level property bag is copied.

elements.title: SSLIntDictionary
elements.summary: Stores values by whole-number keys.
caveats: AddValue() and GetValue() are forgiving with invalid keys, but Contains(), Remove(), and TryGetValue() are not.
GetValue() uses the optional fallback only when no value is returned for the key.

do: Use SSLIntDictionary when your keys are integer-valued numeric IDs such as sample, order, or task identifiers.
Use TryGetValue() when you need to know whether a key exists without relying on a fallback value.
Pass an initial capacity only when you already expect a larger dictionary.
dont: Pass text or fractional values as keys, because invalid keys either fail silently with .F., return the fallback value, or raise an error, depending on the method you call.
Use GetValue() by itself when you must tell the difference between a missing key and a stored value that could match your fallback.
Assume adding the same key creates a second entry, because AddValue() replaces the existing value for that key.

elements.title: SSLRegex
elements.summary: Matches SSL strings against a stored regular expression pattern.
caveats: Passing nStartAt beyond the end of the string does not raise an error; matching starts at the end of the input.

do: Reuse one SSLRegex instance when you need the same pattern for multiple values.
Pass .F. explicitly when matching should ignore letter case.
Use a 1-based nStartAt only when you intentionally want to skip earlier characters.
dont: Pass an empty or invalid pattern unless you want the regex engine's behavior exactly as written. Invalid patterns fail at object creation.
Rely on the default constructor when case-insensitive matching is required. Use SSLRegex{sPattern, .F.} so the behavior is explicit.
Pass 0 or a negative nStartAt. Those values raise a runtime error instead of returning .F..

elements.title: SSLSQLError
elements.summary: Represents the SQL-specific error object returned after a database failure.
caveats: Description and ErrorMessage are the same value, and inherited Code returns the same numeric value as GenCode.
ErrorMessage and ErrorStackTrace can include text from nested causes in the same exception chain.
Inherited SSLError properties such as FullDescription, FullDescriptionEx, NETException, and InnerException are still available.

do: Use oErr:Description or oErr:ErrorMessage for the primary SQL error text.
Check oErr:SQLState, oErr:GenCode, and oErr:Sql together when you need structured diagnostics.
Use inherited members such as oErr:Code, oErr:FullDescription, or oErr:FullDescriptionEx when support logging needs more context.
dont: Assume oErr:SQLState uses the same text format for every provider.
Treat oErr:GenCode as an independently stored database field. SSL derives it from SQLState.
Show raw SQL text or stack traces directly to end users unless that level of technical detail is intentional.

elements.title: SSLStringDictionary
elements.summary: Stores values by string key.
caveats: Contains(), Remove(), and TryGetValue() are stricter than AddValue() and GetValue() for invalid keys.
In a case-sensitive dictionary, "Spec" and "spec" are different keys.
The nLength argument is only a starting-size hint. It does not limit how many entries the dictionary can hold.

do: Use the default constructor when you want case-insensitive lookups for ordinary status names, setting names, or other user-facing keys.
Use TryGetValue() when you need to know whether a key exists instead of relying on a fallback value.
Pass a capacity hint only when you already expect a larger dictionary.
dont: Assume different casing creates separate entries in the default constructor, because SSLStringDictionary{} matches keys case-insensitively.
Use GetValue() by itself when a stored value could be the same as your fallback, because only TryGetValue() tells you whether the key was present.
Pass NIL, numbers, or other non-string values as keys, because invalid keys either fail silently with .F., return the fallback value, or raise an error, depending on the method you call.

elements.title: Sequence
elements.summary: Creates and manages a database sequence for a table field on Oracle or SQL Server.
caveats: SequenceName is derived from the table name and field name, both converted to uppercase, and always starts with C_. When a prefix is supplied, it is appended in uppercase after the field name.
Create() handles create failures by showing a message rather than exposing a dedicated return value.
Drop(), Exists, NextValue, and Reset() still depend on the target database being reachable and the sequence being valid in that database.
Oracle and SQL Server do not reset sequences the same way. Oracle reset recreates the sequence, while SQL Server resets the existing sequence in place.
Very long Oracle-derived sequence names may not remain human-readable because the name is shortened before use.

do: Set StartWith before Create() when the sequence must begin at a known value.
Check Exists before calling Drop() or before assuming NextValue is available.
Use SetDatabase() before sequence operations when the work should target a non-default database.
dont: Assume CacheSize changes Oracle behavior, because it is only used when SQL Server creates the sequence.
Treat any non-"ORACLE" platform value as validation-safe input, because it falls back to SQL Server behavior.
Use Reset() on Oracle when the sequence cannot be safely recreated, because Oracle does not support resetting in place and the operation resets the sequence by recreating it.

elements.title: TablesImport
elements.summary: Loads one imported table at a time from a folder structure and returns it as a CDataTable.
caveats: GetTable() only checks the conventional import path <sFolder>/<sName>/<sName>.txt.
NullAsBlank is writable on the class, but GetTable() does not use it.
Passing NIL for sName raises an error instead of returning NIL and setting ErrMsg.
IncludeORIGREC only affects the returned table when the imported data actually contains an ORIGREC column.

do: Check the return value from GetTable() before using the table.
Read ErrMsg when GetTable() returns NIL.
Set IncludeORIGREC before loading tables if your script needs to keep or remove that column consistently.
dont: Assume the table loaded successfully. Missing files, unreadable files, and invalid import content cause GetTable() to return NIL.
Rely on NullAsBlank to change imported values. This class accepts the property, but GetTable() does not use it.
Pass NIL as the table name. That raises an error instead of returning NIL.

elements.title: WebServices
elements.summary: Creates client objects for outbound HTTP and SOAP integrations.
caveats: This page documents the WebServices factory class only. The returned HTTP and SOAP client objects have their own APIs that are not described here.

do: Use CreateHttpClient() for HTTP-based integrations.
Use CreateSoapClient() for SOAP-based integrations.
Treat each factory call as returning a new client object.
dont: Call CreateSoapClient() when you need an HTTP client.
Call CreateHttpClient() when you need a SOAP client.
Assume this page documents the full API of the returned client objects.

elements.title: BEGINCASE
elements.summary: Starts a :BEGINCASE block for evaluating one or more boolean :CASE conditions, with an optional :OTHERWISE branch and a required :ENDCASE.
caveats: SSL keywords are case-sensitive. Write :BEGINCASE, :CASE, :OTHERWISE, :EXITCASE, and :ENDCASE in uppercase.

do: End each :CASE and :OTHERWISE branch with :EXITCASE; unless you intentionally want multi-match behavior.
Add an :OTHERWISE; branch when the logic needs a clear default path.
Use :BEGINCASE; when several boolean conditions are easier to read as branches than as a long :IF chain.
dont: Assume :BEGINCASE; stops after the first true branch. Without :EXITCASE;, later :CASE expressions are still evaluated and more than one body can run.
Omit :OTHERWISE; when the block needs explicit default handling.
Use :BEGINCASE; for trivial two-way branching where :IF and :ELSE would be clearer.

elements.title: BEGININLINECODE
elements.summary: Starts a named inline SSL code block that is stored for later retrieval with GetInlineCode.
caveats: GetInlineCode raises a runtime error if the named block is not in scope.
DeleteInlineCode raises a runtime error if the named block is not in scope.

do: Use a clear block name that matches how the code will be retrieved.
Keep the inline body valid SSL so the script compiles cleanly.
Delete temporary inline code with DeleteInlineCode after use when the block is no longer needed.
dont: Treat the block name as a language name; it is a lookup name, not a host-language selector.
Use :BEGININLINECODE for unvalidated free-form text. Use REGION when you need stored text that is not validated as executable SSL.
Omit :ENDINLINECODE or the required block name; the script will not compile.

elements.title: CASE
elements.summary: Executes a block of statements if a specific boolean expression evaluates to true within a CASE structure.

do: Write clear boolean expressions for each :CASE branch.
End each :CASE body with :EXITCASE unless you intentionally want later matching branches to run.
Include :OTHERWISE when the structure needs a default path.
dont: Assume :BEGINCASE behaves like a traditional switch statement. Each :CASE uses its own boolean expression, not a shared value match.
Omit :EXITCASE by accident. Without it, later :CASE expressions are still evaluated and additional matching bodies can run.
Use expressions that may fail logical conversion at runtime.

elements.title: CATCH
elements.summary: Handles errors raised in the immediately preceding :TRY block.
caveats: :CATCH cannot appear outside a :TRY block.
A :CATCH block may be empty, but the preceding :TRY body must contain at least one statement.
:CATCH handles all errors from that :TRY block; you cannot declare multiple typed catches.
If the :TRY block has no :CATCH, the error propagates unless another outer handler intercepts it.
Keywords are case-sensitive and must be written in uppercase.

do: Keep :CATCH focused on the specific work performed in the preceding :TRY block.
Retrieve the current error with GetLastSSLError and use oErr:Description when you need message text.
Put cleanup that must run on both success and failure in :FINALLY.
dont: Use one :CATCH block to handle unrelated work from distant parts of a procedure — keep it paired closely with its :TRY so the recovery logic stays predictable.
Rely on :CATCH alone for required cleanup. If no error occurs, :CATCH is skipped, but :FINALLY still runs.

elements.title: CLASS
elements.summary: Defines a class in SSL. A class file can declare fields, methods, an optional base class, and a Constructor procedure for instance initialization.
caveats: Members whose names start with _ are excluded from reflection-based access.

do: Put :INHERIT immediately after :CLASS when the class derives from
Keep class fields in :DECLARE statements near the top of the file so
Place regular methods before Constructor and keep the constructor last for predictable structure.
Instantiate user-defined classes with CreateUdObject instead of built-in-class curly-brace syntax.
Use Me: for sibling method calls and Base: for inherited behavior inside class methods.
dont: Mix script logic and a class definition in the same file. :CLASS runs
Use ClassName{} syntax for a user-defined class — that pattern targets
Return a value from Constructor. SSL treats that as a compile-time error.
Split related fields across method bodies. Declaring them in the class body makes the instance state clear and consistent.

elements.title: DECLARE
elements.summary: Declares one or more variables in the current SSL scope.
caveats: :DECLARE does not assign a custom initial value. If you need one, declare the name and then assign it on a later statement.
All colon-prefixed keywords are case-sensitive, so write :DECLARE in uppercase.

do: Declare variables before their first use.
Group related declarations near the start of a routine when that improves readability.
Use clear Hungarian-style names for local variables in examples and new code.
dont: Rely on assignment to introduce a variable. SSL expects names to be declared before use.
Re-declare the same name casually. SSL accepts it, but repeated declarations make scope harder to follow.
Scatter declarations through unrelated logic just because the language permits it. Use later declarations only when they genuinely improve clarity.

elements.title: DEFAULT
elements.summary: Assigns a fallback expression to a parameter when the caller omits that argument.
caveats: Colon-prefixed keywords are case-sensitive, so write :DEFAULT in uppercase.

do: Use :DEFAULT for optional parameters that have a clear, stable fallback value.
Keep :DEFAULT lines directly under :PARAMETERS so the routine interface is easy to read.
Prefer simple fallback expressions that make the omitted-argument behavior obvious.
dont: Use :DEFAULT as a replacement for normal assignments later in the routine. It only belongs in the parameter setup section.
Scatter :DEFAULT lines after :DECLARE or executable statements. That placement does not match SSL syntax.
Hide important business decisions inside surprising defaults. If a default changes behavior in a meaningful way, document it clearly.

elements.title: ELSE
elements.summary: Directs control to an alternate set of statements when the preceding IF condition is false.
caveats: Keywords in SSL are case-sensitive and must be written in uppercase.

do: Use :ELSE to clearly define mutually exclusive logic paths.
Always keep :ELSE branches as minimal as necessary.
dont: Nest :IF blocks in the :ELSE section when simple two-way branching is sufficient. Clarity is improved and branching logic stays concise.
Place unrelated logic within :ELSE just to re-use the block. Each branch should address one logical alternative for easier understanding and maintenance.

elements.title: ENDCASE
elements.summary: Closes a :BEGINCASE block after its :CASE branches and optional :OTHERWISE branch.
caveats: :OTHERWISE is optional, but if present it must appear before :ENDCASE;.
CASE keywords are case-sensitive and must be uppercase.

do: End every :BEGINCASE block with a matching :ENDCASE;.
Place :ENDCASE; after all :CASE branches and any optional :OTHERWISE branch.
Keep :ENDCASE; paired with :EXITCASE in branch bodies when you want single-branch behavior.
dont: Write :ENDCASE; outside a :BEGINCASE block.
Omit :ENDCASE; and expect SSL to close the CASE block implicitly.
Assume :ENDCASE; alone prevents later :CASE branches from running. Use :EXITCASE for that.

elements.title: ENDIF
elements.summary: Terminates an :IF block, with or without an :ELSE branch.

do: Always pair each :IF with a matching :ENDIF;, including nested conditionals.
Keep the :IF, optional :ELSE, and :ENDIF; visibly aligned so the full block is easy to follow.
dont: Omit :ENDIF; and rely on indentation or spacing to imply the end of the block. SSL requires an explicit closing keyword.
Mis-case the keyword as :endif or :EndIf. Colon-prefixed SSL keywords are case-sensitive and must be uppercase.

elements.title: ENDINLINECODE
elements.summary: Ends a named inline SSL code block opened by :BEGININLINECODE.
caveats: The block body before :ENDINLINECODE; must be valid SSL.
Use REGION instead when you need stored text that is not validated as executable SSL.

do: Close every :BEGININLINECODE block explicitly with :ENDINLINECODE;.
Put :ENDINLINECODE; on its own line so the block boundary is obvious.
Keep the inline body valid SSL so the script compiles cleanly.
dont: Omit :ENDINLINECODE; — the inline block must be closed explicitly.
Use :ENDINLINECODE; without a matching :BEGININLINECODE — the script will not compile.
Treat :ENDINLINECODE; as an executable statement with arguments. It only closes the inline block.

elements.title: ENDPROC
elements.summary: Terminates a procedure block and signals the end of its executable statements.
caveats: Constructors also end with :ENDPROC; because they are declared with :PROCEDURE Constructor; syntax.
SSL keywords are case-sensitive, so :endproc is not valid syntax.

do: End every :PROCEDURE block with :ENDPROC;.
Use :RETURN for early exits or returned values.
Keep procedure boundaries visually clear with consistent indentation and spacing.
dont: Omit :ENDPROC; or rely on an implicit end to the procedure.
Use :ENDPROC as a substitute for :RETURN when you need to exit early.
Place extra procedure-body statements after the closing :ENDPROC;.

elements.title: ENDREGION
elements.summary: Keyword that marks the end of a :REGION block; it has no standalone runtime behavior.
caveats: SSL keywords are case-sensitive, so :endregion is not valid syntax.

do: Close every :REGION block with a matching :ENDREGION; before returning to normal script statements.
Use a valid identifier in the opening :REGION name so the region can be retrieved reliably with GetRegion().
dont: Treat :ENDREGION; as executable logic. It only closes a region capture started by :REGION.
Use :REGION and :ENDREGION when you only want editor folding. Prefer /* region and /* endregion comments for code organization.

elements.title: ENDTRY
elements.summary: Closes a structured :TRY block after its :CATCH and/or :FINALLY sections.
caveats: :ENDTRY must be written in uppercase as a colon-prefixed keyword.

do: Always include :ENDTRY whenever you create a :TRY block, regardless of whether you use :CATCH or :FINALLY sections.
Place :ENDTRY only after all associated :CATCH and :FINALLY sections.
Format :ENDTRY clearly and align it with its opening :TRY for readability.
dont: Omit :ENDTRY, expecting the block to close automatically. Omitting :ENDTRY causes compile-time errors and leaves error handling incomplete.
Insert :ENDTRY before these sections or outside the :TRY block. Early or misplaced :ENDTRY disrupts the intended flow and terminates exception handling prematurely.
Scatter related error-handling keywords, making the block hard to follow. Consistent structure improves code clarity and maintainability.

elements.title: ENDWHILE
elements.summary: Closes a :WHILE loop block.
caveats: :ENDWHILE closes a :WHILE block; it is not used for :FOR loops.
:ENDWHILE must be written as an uppercase colon-prefixed keyword.

do: Pair each :ENDWHILE with the :WHILE it closes.
Keep repeated work inside the loop body, before :ENDWHILE;.
Align :ENDWHILE with its matching :WHILE to make nesting clear.
dont: Treat :ENDWHILE as a standalone statement unrelated to a :WHILE block. It is a structural closer, not an independent control-flow action.
Place statements after :ENDWHILE; if they must run on every iteration. Code after the closer runs only after the loop exits.

elements.title: ERROR
elements.summary: Defines a legacy error handler for the statements that follow it in the current procedure or method.
caveats: :ERROR is legacy scope-based handling for the current procedure or method. It is not a clause inside :TRY.
:RESUME changes the flow to resume-mode handling for subsequent statements. Use it only when continuing is safe.
If no handled error occurs, the :ERROR body is skipped.

do: Use :ERROR only for legacy handler flows that genuinely need :ERROR / :RESUME.
Put the :ERROR block before the statements it is meant to protect.
Use GetLastSSLError() and oErr:Description inside the handler when you need a readable error message.
Prefer :TRY / :CATCH / :FINALLY for new code.
dont: Describe :ERROR as part of :TRY / :CATCH / :FINALLY. It is a separate legacy mechanism.
Use :ERROR for new structured exception-handling examples when :TRY / :CATCH would be clearer.
Assume :RESUME is optional when the procedure is meant to continue after failures. Without it, the handler does not provide resume-mode recovery.

elements.title: EXITCASE
elements.summary: Ends the current :CASE or :OTHERWISE branch and continues after :ENDCASE.
caveats: SSL keywords are case-sensitive. Write :EXITCASE in uppercase.

do: End each :CASE and :OTHERWISE branch with :EXITCASE; unless you intentionally want later matching branches to run.
Use :EXITCASE; to make single-branch :BEGINCASE behavior explicit.
Keep :EXITCASE; on its own line at the end of the branch body.
dont: Omit :EXITCASE; by accident. Without it, later :CASE expressions are still evaluated and more than one branch body may run.
Place statements after :EXITCASE; in the same branch. That code is unreachable and makes the block harder to read.
Use :EXITCASE; outside a :BEGINCASE structure.

elements.title: EXITFOR
elements.summary: Terminates the innermost active :FOR loop immediately and continues with the statement after the matching :NEXT.
caveats: :EXITFOR must be written in uppercase.
Outside a :FOR loop, :EXITFOR raises Found :EXITFOR outside :FOR.
Inside a :FINALLY block, :EXITFOR raises Cannot have :EXITFOR inside :FINALLY.

do: Use :EXITFOR to stop a :FOR loop once the target condition is met.
Keep the exit condition explicit with :IF so the early exit is easy to read.
Preserve any state the caller needs after the loop, such as a found flag or result value.
dont: Use :EXITFOR outside a :FOR loop. That raises Found :EXITFOR outside :FOR.
Place :EXITFOR in a :FINALLY block. That raises Cannot have :EXITFOR inside :FINALLY.
Use :EXITFOR as a substitute for ordinary branching when the loop should continue running.

elements.title: EXITWHILE
elements.summary: Terminates the innermost active :WHILE loop and continues with the statement after the matching :ENDWHILE.
caveats: :EXITWHILE must be written as an uppercase colon-prefixed keyword.
Outside a :WHILE loop, :EXITWHILE raises Found :EXITWHILE outside :WHILE.
Inside a :FINALLY block, :EXITWHILE raises Cannot have :EXITWHILE inside :FINALLY.

do: Use :EXITWHILE to stop a :WHILE loop once the loop's goal has been reached.
Keep the exit condition explicit with :IF so the early exit is easy to follow.
Preserve any state the caller needs after the loop, such as a found flag or error message.
dont: Use :EXITWHILE outside a :WHILE loop. That raises Found :EXITWHILE outside :WHILE.
Place :EXITWHILE in a :FINALLY block. That raises Cannot have :EXITWHILE inside :FINALLY.
Use :EXITWHILE as a substitute for ordinary loop conditions when the loop should exit naturally.

elements.title: FINALLY
elements.summary: Starts the cleanup section of a :TRY block and always runs after the protected work completes.
caveats: :FINALLY is only valid inside a :TRY structure.
:FINALLY must appear after the :TRY body and before :ENDTRY;.
When both blocks are present, :CATCH must come before :FINALLY.
Keywords are case-sensitive and must be written in uppercase.

do: Use :FINALLY for cleanup that must happen whether the :TRY body succeeds or fails.
Keep :FINALLY focused on cleanup, state restoration, and other mandatory end-of-block work.
Put error-specific recovery in :CATCH and shared cleanup in :FINALLY when you need both.
dont: Put :RETURN, :EXITWHILE, :EXITFOR, or :LOOP inside :FINALLY. All four forms are invalid there.
Move main business logic into :FINALLY. That block should stay predictable and cleanup-oriented.
Leave a :FINALLY body empty. It must contain at least one statement.

elements.title: FOR
elements.summary: Executes a counted loop by assigning a numeric variable, checking it against a numeric limit, and updating it after each iteration.
caveats: Leaving out the required closing :NEXT; causes a parse error.
:EXITFOR; outside a :FOR loop causes a compile-time error with the message Found :EXITFOR outside :FOR.
The start, limit, and step values must be numeric at runtime.
If the start value is already past the limit for the chosen step direction, the loop body does not run.
A step of 0 prevents the loop variable from advancing, which can create a non-terminating loop when the initial condition is true.

do: Use :STEP only when you need an increment other than the default 1.
Use distinct loop variables in nested loops so the control flow stays readable.
dont: Omit the closing :NEXT; or use :EXITFOR; outside a :FOR ... :NEXT block.
Rely on changes to the original limit or step expressions inside the loop body.

elements.title: IF
elements.summary: Executes a block of statements only when a condition evaluates to true.
caveats: :IF, :ELSE, and :ENDIF are case-sensitive SSL keywords and must be written in uppercase with the leading colon.

do: Carefully design your condition to be explicit and unambiguous.
Always close each :IF with a matching :ENDIF and use :ELSE when alternate branches are needed.
Limit each :IF block to a single logical decision when possible.
dont: Write complex nested conditions that are difficult to read or debug. Clear conditions reduce maintenance complexity and misinterpretation.
Omit :ENDIF or misalign :ENDIF and :ELSE, which can cause unreachable code or errors.
Overload a single :IF with unrelated checks connected by complex boolean logic. Keeping blocks focused improves clarity and simplifies future changes.

elements.title: INCLUDE
elements.summary: Inserts another SSL script's source into the current file so its statements compile as if they were written inline.
caveats: :INCLUDE must be written in uppercase with the leading colon.
The target name must use identifier syntax, not a quoted string.
If the target script cannot be loaded, compilation fails.

do: Place :INCLUDE near the top of the file so the inserted declarations and procedures are available before later code depends on them.
Use includes for shared top-level SSL code such as helper procedures, declarations, or common setup.
Keep include targets explicit and stable so other developers can tell where shared behavior comes from.
dont: Treat :INCLUDE like a runtime call or a namespace import. It inserts source text directly into the current script for compilation.
Scatter include directives deep inside procedural logic unless you have a specific structural reason. Early placement is easier to read and reason about.
Rely on repeated or circular includes as a control mechanism. Later repeats are ignored while compiling a script, which can hide the real source of missing declarations.

elements.title: INHERIT
elements.summary: Specifies the parent class for an SSL class.
caveats: :INHERIT is valid only inside a :CLASS definition.
SSL keywords are case-sensitive, so write :INHERIT in uppercase.

do: Place :INHERIT immediately after :CLASS ClassName;.
Use a plain or qualified parent class name such as BaseValidator or Lab.BaseValidator.
Use Base:MethodName() in child methods when you want to extend, not replace, inherited behavior.
dont: Put :DECLARE statements or methods before :INHERIT because SSL only accepts :INHERIT directly after the class declaration.
Use inheritance just to reach unrelated data or helper methods because it creates fragile class hierarchies.

elements.title: LABEL
elements.summary: Marks a named location in a procedure or script for control flow redirection.
caveats: :LABEL name; and :LABELname; are different stored texts and require different Branch() targets.
Keyword names are case-sensitive and must be uppercase: use :LABEL, not :label.

do: Choose descriptive labels such as CLEANUP or RECOVER that make the jump target obvious.
Pass the exact stored target text to Branch(), such as Branch("LABEL CLEANUP") for :LABEL CLEANUP;.
Reserve labels for shared recovery or cleanup paths when structured flow would be less clear.
dont: Pass only the trailing name, such as "CLEANUP", for a normal :LABEL CLEANUP; declaration. The stored text includes LABEL.
Replace ordinary :IF, loop, or :RETURN flow with labels when a structured construct is sufficient.
Use vague names such as L1 or NEXT that make jump targets harder to follow.

elements.title: LOOP
elements.summary: Skips the rest of the current :WHILE or :FOR iteration and continues with the next iteration of the innermost active loop.
caveats: :LOOP must be written as an uppercase colon-prefixed keyword.

do: Use :LOOP for guard-style early skips when the loop should continue.
Place :LOOP immediately after the condition that makes the current item invalid or irrelevant.
Keep required per-iteration state updates before :LOOP, or in a place that still runs on skipped iterations.
dont: Use :EXITFOR or :EXITWHILE when only the current iteration should be skipped. Those keywords terminate the loop instead of continuing it.
Place :LOOP inside a :FINALLY block. That raises Cannot have :LOOP inside :FINALLY.
Place :LOOP where required cleanup or bookkeeping would be skipped unintentionally.
Assume code after :LOOP still runs for that iteration. It never does.

elements.title: NEXT
elements.summary: Closes a :FOR loop and returns control to the loop's increment-and-test step.
caveats: :NEXT; must be written as an uppercase colon-prefixed keyword.
Code after :NEXT; runs only after the loop finishes, not after each iteration.
Use :EXITFOR; to leave a loop early and :LOOP; to skip to the next iteration. :NEXT; serves a different role.

do: Place :NEXT; immediately after the full body of the :FOR loop it closes.
Keep the loop body indented consistently so the matching :FOR and :NEXT; are easy to read.
Put statements that should run once after the loop below :NEXT;, not inside the loop body.
dont: Treat :NEXT; like :LOOP; or :EXITFOR;. :NEXT; closes the loop block; it does not skip or terminate an iteration early.
Omit :NEXT; from a :FOR loop. SSL requires it to close the block.
Place unrelated statements between the intended end of the loop body and :NEXT; in a way that makes the loop boundary unclear.

elements.title: OTHERWISE
elements.summary: Executes the default branch of a :BEGINCASE block when no earlier :CASE branch runs.
caveats: :BEGINCASE requires at least one :CASE branch before :OTHERWISE can appear.
A :BEGINCASE block can contain at most one :OTHERWISE branch.
Keywords are case-sensitive, so write :OTHERWISE in uppercase.

do: Add an :OTHERWISE branch when unmatched inputs still need defined behavior.
Keep :OTHERWISE after all :CASE branches and usually end its body with :EXITCASE; for consistency with the rest of the :BEGINCASE block.
dont: Put :OTHERWISE before a later :CASE branch or outside a :BEGINCASE block.
Use :OTHERWISE as a substitute for a missing condition when a normal :CASE branch would describe the logic more clearly.

elements.title: PARAMETERS
elements.summary: Declares named input parameters for a script, procedure, method, or constructor.
caveats: Use at most one :PARAMETERS statement in a given script, procedure, method, or constructor body.
A standard :PARAMETERS statement must include at least one parameter name.
In standard SSL, :DEFAULT lines must immediately follow :PARAMETERS when used.
In SSL and SQL data source files, every parameter must include an inline default value with :=.
Colon-prefixed keywords are case-sensitive, so write :PARAMETERS in uppercase.

do: Put :PARAMETERS first in the routine body whenever the routine accepts arguments.
Keep parameter names stable and descriptive because callers pass values positionally.
Keep parameter lists focused, and move optional trailing defaults to :DEFAULT lines in standard SSL.
dont: Write :PARAMETERS after :DECLARE, executable statements, or control flow. That placement is invalid in standard SSL bodies.
Use :PARAMETERS as a substitute for local-variable declarations. Local working names belong in :DECLARE.
Use standard :PARAMETERS plus separate :DEFAULT lines in data source files. Data sources require inline := defaults on the :PARAMETERS line.

elements.title: PROCEDURE
elements.summary: Declares a named routine body that can contain executable SSL statements and end with :ENDPROC ;.
caveats: :PROCEDURE is case-sensitive and must be written in uppercase.
:DEFAULT lines belong immediately after :PARAMETERS, not on :DECLARE lines.
More than 20 parameters is allowed, but it produces a performance warning.
Script-level visibility annotations such as /*@private; and /*@protected; apply only to script procedures and must appear immediately before :PROCEDURE.

do: Use a clear PascalCase procedure name that reflects the routine's job.
Put :PARAMETERS first in the body when the routine accepts arguments, and place any :DEFAULT lines immediately after it.
Call same-file script procedures with DoProc ("ProcedureName", {args}), and use :RETURN when the caller needs a result.
dont: Call a custom script procedure directly as ProcedureName(args). Use DoProc for same-file procedures or ExecFunction for external scripts.
Omit :ENDPROC ; or treat it as optional. The procedure body must close explicitly.
Use DoProc inside class methods. Inside a class, call sibling or inherited routines with Me:Method() or Base:Method().

elements.title: PUBLIC
elements.summary: Declares global variables that can be accessed from any scope in the program.
caveats: :PUBLIC is valid in script and procedure statement flow. It is not a class
Public variables start as the empty string "", not NIL.
Colon-prefixed keywords are case-sensitive, so write :PUBLIC in uppercase.

do: Use :PUBLIC sparingly for state that genuinely must be shared across scopes.
Keep public names clear and stable because any scope can read or overwrite them.
Prefer declaring public variables near the workflow entry point so shared state is easy to discover.
dont: Use :PUBLIC for routine-local working values that could stay in :DECLARE or be passed as parameters. That creates hidden coupling.
Assume :PUBLIC is required at the top of the file. SSL allows it later in normal statement flow, so placement is a readability choice.
Reuse a generic public name like sValue or bFlag across unrelated workflows. Shared names are easy to overwrite accidentally.

elements.title: REGION
elements.summary: Starts a named region block whose body is stored as text for later retrieval with GetRegion().
caveats: :REGION must be closed by a matching :ENDREGION;.
:REGION and :ENDREGION are case-sensitive keywords and must be uppercase.
GetRegion() raises an error if the requested region name is not in scope.
Leading and trailing line breaks immediately inside the region body are trimmed before the text is stored.

do: Use :REGION for stored text that you plan to retrieve with GetRegion().
Choose descriptive identifier names so the region's purpose is obvious at the call site.
Keep placeholder tokens consistent when you plan to replace them with GetRegion(sName, aSource, aDestination).
dont: Use quotes around the region name. :REGION takes an identifier, not a string literal.
Expect the region body to execute where it is declared. The body is captured as text and retrieved later.
Use :REGION when you only want code folding. Prefer /* region and /* endregion comments for that purpose.

elements.title: RESUME
elements.summary: Continues execution after a legacy :ERROR handler, starting with the statement after the one that failed.
caveats: :RESUME must appear in a procedure that also contains an :ERROR handler.
:RESUME does not retry the failing statement. Execution continues with the next statement after the error.
If later statements keep failing and the handler does not resolve the problem, the procedure can continue producing repeated errors.

do: Use :RESUME only when the failure is expected and later statements can still run safely.
Keep the :ERROR handler focused on logging, cleanup, or lightweight recovery before :RESUME takes effect.
Prefer :TRY / :CATCH / :FINALLY in new code when you only need to protect a specific block.
dont: Use :RESUME for unknown or non-recoverable errors because that can hide real failures and keep the procedure running in a bad state.
Use :RESUME without an :ERROR handler because the procedure will not compile.
Place :RESUME in :CATCH or :FINALLY blocks because it belongs only to the legacy :ERROR pattern.

elements.title: RETURN
elements.summary: Ends the current script, procedure, or method immediately and can optionally return a value.
caveats: Keywords are case-sensitive and must be written in uppercase.
Placing :RETURN inside :FINALLY is a compile error: Cannot have :RETURN inside :FINALLY.
Using :RETURN value; in a constructor is a compile error: Cannot return values from a constructor.

do: Use :RETURN for clear guard clauses when validation fails early.
Return explicit values when callers depend on a result.
Keep cleanup in :FINALLY and place :RETURN in the :TRY or :CATCH body instead.
dont: Put :RETURN inside :FINALLY. It is invalid there.
Use :RETURN value; inside a constructor. Constructors may only use the bare form.
Leave dead statements after a :RETURN and expect them to run.

elements.title: STEP
elements.summary: Sets the increment or decrement used by a :FOR loop between iterations.
caveats: The step value must be numeric at runtime. Non-numeric values raise the runtime error For stmt: Step value must be a number.
The loop limit must also be numeric. If it is not, SSL raises For stmt: Limit value must be a number.
A step of 0 is accepted, but if the initial loop condition is true, the loop does not progress and can run indefinitely.
:STEP must be uppercase and colon-prefixed.

do: Use :STEP when the loop should move by a value other than the default 1.
Use a negative step explicitly for descending loops.
Guard computed step values when 0 would create a non-terminating loop.
dont: Use :STEP 0 unless the loop body has a deliberate exit path. A zero step does not advance the loop variable.
Assume changing the original step expression inside the loop body changes the current loop. SSL reuses the step value chosen before the body starts.
Use :ENDFOR instead of :NEXT; to close a :FOR loop.

elements.title: TO
elements.summary: Sets the inclusive loop limit used by a :FOR loop.
caveats: :TO is only valid as part of a :FOR header.
The current value must be numeric at runtime or SSL raises For stmt: Current value must be a number.
The limit value must be numeric at runtime or SSL raises For stmt: Limit value must be a number.
The step value must be numeric at runtime or SSL raises For stmt: Step value must be a number.
A step of 0 is accepted, but if the initial loop condition is true, the loop can become non-terminating because the loop variable does not change.
:TO must be uppercase and colon-prefixed.

do: Use :TO with a clear numeric limit so the loop boundary is easy to read.
Pair :TO with a negative :STEP when the loop should count downward.
Validate computed bounds when they come from user input or runtime data.
dont: Use :TO outside a :FOR header.
Assume a zero :STEP is safe. If the initial loop condition is true, the loop variable never advances.
Pass non-numeric values for the current value, limit, or step. SSL raises runtime errors for those cases.

elements.title: TRY
elements.summary: Starts a protected block that can transfer control to :CATCH, :FINALLY, or both when errors occur.
caveats: A :TRY block must include at least one :CATCH or :FINALLY section.
Only one :CATCH block is allowed per :TRY block.
When both are present, :CATCH must come before :FINALLY.
The :TRY body must contain at least one statement.
A :FINALLY block must also contain at least one statement when present.
:CATCH does not declare an exception variable; retrieve the current error with GetLastSSLError().
Keywords are case-sensitive and must be written in uppercase.

do: Keep the :TRY body narrow so the paired :CATCH handles one clear unit of work.
Use GetLastSSLError() inside :CATCH and prefer oErr:Description when you need the message text.
Put shared cleanup in :FINALLY when locks, temporary state, or resources must always be released.
dont: Wrap large unrelated sections of code in one :TRY block. That makes failures harder to understand and recover from.
Expect multiple typed :CATCH blocks. SSL allows only one :CATCH per :TRY.
Put :RETURN, :EXITWHILE, :EXITFOR, or :LOOP inside :FINALLY. Those control-flow statements are invalid there.

elements.title: WHILE
elements.summary: Repeats a block of statements as long as a supplied condition evaluates to true.
caveats: Omitting the matching :ENDWHILE causes a compile-time error.
If the condition does not evaluate to a boolean value at runtime, SSL raises a runtime error.
:EXITWHILE outside a :WHILE block causes a compile-time error:
:LOOP outside a :WHILE or :FOR block causes a compile-time error:
:EXITWHILE and :LOOP are not allowed inside :FINALLY blocks.
Keywords are case-sensitive and must be uppercase, for example :WHILE and :ENDWHILE.

do: Update the condition state inside the loop so progress toward termination is clear.
Use :WHILE when the number of iterations depends on values that change at runtime.
Use :EXITWHILE or :LOOP deliberately when they make the loop flow easier to read.
dont: Rely on a condition that never changes within the block, because that can create an unintended infinite loop.
Use :WHILE when a :FOR loop expresses a fixed-count iteration more clearly.
Use :EXITWHILE or :LOOP outside a loop, or inside a :FINALLY block, because those forms are invalid.

elements.title: add-assign
elements.summary: Updates a variable, property, or array element in place by applying + and then storing the result back into the left side.

elements.title: and
elements.summary: Combines two boolean expressions and returns .T. only when both are .T..

elements.title: assignment
elements.summary: Stores a value in a variable, object property, or array element and evaluates to the assigned value.

elements.title: bang
elements.summary: Performs boolean negation, returning the opposite boolean value.

elements.title: decrement
elements.summary: Decreases a number variable by one, trims trailing spaces in strings, or subtracts days from a date depending on operand type.

elements.title: divide-assign
elements.summary: Divides a numeric value by another numeric value and stores the quotient back in the left operand.

elements.title: divide
elements.summary: Divides the left number by the right number and returns the result as a number.

elements.title: dollar
elements.summary: Tests whether the left string is found within the right operand as a substring.

elements.title: double-star-power
elements.summary: Raises one number to the exponent of another number.

elements.title: equals
elements.summary: Compares two values for equality, using prefix matching for strings, exact matching for numbers, booleans, and dates, and reference equality for arrays and objects.

elements.title: greater-than-or-equal
elements.summary: Compares two values of the same supported type and returns .T. when the left operand is greater than or equal to the right operand.

elements.title: greater-than
elements.summary: Compares two values of the same supported type and returns .T. when the left operand is strictly greater than the right operand.

elements.title: hash
elements.summary: Returns .T. when two values are not equal and .F. when they are equal.

elements.title: increment
elements.summary: Increases a number variable by one, modifying its value in place.

elements.title: less-than-or-equal
elements.summary: Compares two values of the same supported type and returns .T. when the left operand is less than or equal to the right operand.

elements.title: less-than
elements.summary: Compares two values of the same supported type and returns .T. when the left operand is strictly less than the right operand.

elements.title: minus
elements.summary: Subtracts numbers, trims trailing spaces before string concatenation, or performs date arithmetic depending on operand types.

elements.title: modulo-assign
elements.summary: Updates a numeric variable, property, or array element in place by applying % and storing the remainder back into the left side.

elements.title: modulo
elements.summary: Calculates the remainder after dividing one number by another.

elements.title: multiply-assign
elements.summary: Multiplies a numeric value by another numeric value and stores the product back in the left operand.

elements.title: multiply
elements.summary: Multiplies one number by another number and returns the product.

elements.title: not-equals-legacy
elements.summary: Returns .T. when two values are not equal under SSL's strict inequality rules.

elements.title: not-equals
elements.summary: Returns .T. when two values are not strictly equal under SSL equality rules.

elements.title: not
elements.summary: Performs boolean negation, returning .T. for .F. and .F. for .T..

elements.title: or
elements.summary: Combines two boolean expressions and returns .T. when either operand is .T..

elements.title: plus
elements.summary: Adds numbers, concatenates strings, or adds days to dates depending on operand types.

elements.title: power-assign
elements.summary: Raises a numeric value to a numeric power and stores the result back into the left side.

elements.title: power
elements.summary: Raises one number to the exponent of another number.

elements.title: shift-left
elements.summary: Shifts the bits of one integer number to the left by the number of positions specified by another integer number.

elements.title: shift-right
elements.summary: Shifts the bits of one integer number to the right by the number of positions specified by another integer number.

elements.title: strict-equals
elements.summary: Returns .T. when two values are strictly equal under SSL equality rules.

elements.title: subtract-assign
elements.summary: Updates a variable, property, or array element in place by applying - and then storing the result back into the left side.

elements.title: false
elements.summary: The .F. literal represents the boolean value false in SSL. It can be written as .F. or .f. (case-insensitive). Use .F. to initialize boolean flags, to explicitly pass a false argument to a function, or to represent a negative or disabled state.

elements.title: nil
elements.summary: NIL is the SSL literal for an explicit absent value. It is case-insensitive, so NIL, nil, and Nil are all valid spellings.

elements.title: true
elements.summary: The .T. literal represents the boolean value true in SSL and is case-insensitive.

elements.title: array
elements.summary: Represents ordered, 1-based collections of SSL values, including nested arrays.

elements.title: boolean
elements.summary: Represents logical true/false values in SSL.

elements.title: codeblock
elements.summary: Represents a callable expression value that you can store, pass, and evaluate.

elements.title: date
elements.summary: The date type represents calendar-based values in SSL. Use it for date arithmetic, ordering, formatting, and serialization without converting values to strings first.

elements.title: netobject
elements.summary: Provides dynamic access to external objects for property access, method invocation, and selected interop scenarios in SSL.

elements.title: number
elements.summary: The number type represents numeric values in SSL. Use it for arithmetic, ordering, exact numeric comparisons, shifts, and integer-only bitwise work.

elements.title: object
elements.summary: Represents SSL object values, including dynamic objects and class instances.

elements.title: string
elements.summary: Represents SSL text values, including string literals, comparisons, indexing, and JSON serialization.

elements.title: Access Modifiers
elements.summary: SSL supports two comment-based access modifiers that control procedure visibility. Place one on the line immediately before a :PROCEDURE declaration.

elements.title: base
elements.summary: Provides explicit access to members on a class's immediate parent type from within a class method.

elements.title: code-block
elements.summary: Defines an anonymous code block with bound variables and a single expression body. A code block can be created at the top level, passed as a function argument, or assigned to a variable for later execution.

elements.title: Code Organization
elements.summary: Comment regions group related procedures or code sections in long files. They are purely organizational - they have no effect on compilation or execution, but development tools use them for code folding and navigation.

elements.title: constructor
elements.summary: Runs one-time class initialization code when a user-defined class instance is created.

elements.title: me
elements.summary: Provides a reference to the current class instance inside :CLASS methods.
